entry.title: Archive Business Process Home Page Cards with BPE Errors
entry.content:
Hello again, community!

I have only asked questions before, but today I feel like this topic, regarding a situation that happened to me while trying to clear stale BPE errors, deserves to be my very first post in the SAP Community.

As we all know, SAP is introducing for SAP SuccessFactors Onboarding, starting with the 1H2026 release, business process tasks that appear in For You Today category within the Needs Attention section on the latest Home Page, instead of appearing only in the to-do panel. But what happens when there are stale BPE errors that need to be cleaned up?

Well, before the 1H2026 release, the answer would have been to simply go to the Check Tool and run the ONB2BPEStaleTodoCleanUp check. In fact, that's exactly what I did before considering the new Home Page Business Cards. By running this check, the tasks disappeared from both the to-do panel and BPE: Manage User Tasks. So, what's the problem with that, you might be wondering? Well, when I noticed that the BPE error cards were still showing up on the latest Home Page, I realized that this check doesn't take the new Home Page cards into account. It only clears the BPE errors in the to-do panel.

So, this post is about that.

With the help of the SAP Support team, a workaround was found. It basically consists of looking up the bpUserTaskId, usually found in the URL when opening the BPE error. Then, go to the BPE: Manage User Tasks tool in Admin Center, filter using the copied User Task ID, select the result, and complete the task from within that tool, this way, the cards on the latest home page can be actually cleared. 

This process needs to be followed in that particular order. Running the ONB2BPEStaleTodoCleanUp check in the Check Tool first makes the processes disappear from the BPE: Manage User Tasks tool, making them unavailable to be archived afterward.

So, that's it! Let me know your thoughts on this workaround, and let's hope SAP comes up with an official solution eventually.

Regards from Mexico! 🎡

entry.title: Principal Propagation from BTP Web App to S/4HANA via SAP Integration Suite
entry.content:
Introduction

During a recent customer PoC, I worked on a scenario where a BTP-hosted web application needed to call an S/4HANA on-premise API — not as a generic service account, but as the actual logged-in user. 
This is where Principal Propagation becomes essential. Preserving user identity end-to-end isn't just a technical nicety — it matters for auditability, compliance, and access control. When S/4HANA receives a call as the real user, every action is traceable: security audit logs show who did what, authorizations are enforced per user, and there's no shared service account masking individual activity.

In this post, I share the configuration and lessons learned that made it work—from a BTP web application authenticated via SAP Cloud Identity Services, through SAP Integration Suite and Cloud Connector, all the way to S/4HANA, with user identity preserved throughout the entire chain.

Thanks to @cj_lo  for preparing Cloud Connector with S/HANA On-premise demo system, testing, and troubleshoot together.

Scenario

A simple web application is deployed on SAP BTP Cloud Foundry. When the user clicks a button, it triggers an iFlow on SAP Integration Suite that fetches data from S/4HANA. The S/4HANA system must receive the call as the authenticated user — not as a technical service account. 
While direct access to S/4HANA via a BTP Destination and SAP Cloud Connector is possible, many organizations choose to introduce a middleware layer to support centralized governance, security, monitoring, and error handling. Ultimately, the target architecture is customer-specific and should balance integration requirements, operational considerations, governance needs, and cost.

Browser (logged-in user)
  → BTP Web App (via Approuter + Cloud Identity Services)
    → BTP Destination Service
      → SAP Integration Suite iFlow
        → Cloud Connector
          → S/4HANA (on-premise, as the user)

Prerequisites & Scope

This article assumes the following components are already installed and configured:

  • SAP BTP Cloud Foundry environment
  • SAP Integration Suite
  • SAP Cloud Connector connected to the SAP BTP subaccount
  • SAP Cloud Identity Services – Identity Authentication (IAS) as the Identity Provider
  • SAP S/4HANA system accessible through Cloud Connector
  • User identities mapped between IAS and SAP S/4HANA

This article focuses on the principal propagation scenario from a SAP BTP application through SAP Integration Suite to an on-premise SAP S/4HANA system. Topics such as SAP Cloud Connector installation, SAP BTP onboarding and trust configuration, access control setup, certificate management, and SAP S/4HANA user provisioning are outside the scope of this article. If I were to cover all of them here, I would end up writing a book instead of a mini blog 😉

Relevant SAP documentation is listed in the References section at the end of this article.

Solution Overview

The solution relies on three key components working together:

ComponentRole
ApprouterHandles IAS login, holds the user's JWT token
BTP Destination (OAuth2JWTBearer)Exchanges the user's IAS JWT for an Integration Suite-scoped token using the PI Runtime service key
iFlowReceives the user-scoped token, calls S/4HANA via Cloud Connector with Principal Propagation

The critical insight is using OAuth2JWTBearer as the destination authentication type. This is the only type that correctly takes the user's IAS JWT and exchanges it at the PI Runtime token endpoint — preserving the user identity all the way to SAP Integration Suite.

Configuration

The BTP app consists of the following files — each is covered in the steps below:

your-app/
├── manifest.yml
├── package.json
├── xs-app.json
├── xs-security-ias.json
└── dist/
    └── index.html

Provision BTP Cloud Foundry Services

Before deploying the app, create the required service instances in your Cloud Foundry (CF) space.
Below using CF CLI, if you need a crash course using it, please check out this tutorial here.

Target your CF API endpoint and log in (in this example my region is jp10, yours could be different):

cf api https://api.cf.jp10.hana.ondemand.com
cf login

Create a Destination service instance (used by the Approuter to resolve BTP destinations), you can replace iflow-pp-destination-svc with service name of your choice:

cf create-service destination lite iflow-pp-destination-svc

Create an IAS (Identity) service instance using the xs-security-ias.json security descriptor, you can replace iflow-ias-svc with service name of your choice:

cf create-service identity application iflow-pp-ias-svc -c xs-security-ias.json

Where xs-security-ias.json contains:

{
  "xsappname": "iflow-pp-ias",
  "tenant-mode": "dedicated",
  "credential-type": "X509_GENERATED",
  "oauth2-configuration": {
    "redirect-uris": [
      "https://*.cfapps.jp10.hana.ondemand.com/login/callback?authType=ias"
    ],
    "post-logout-redirect-uris": [
      "https://*.cfapps.jp10.hana.ondemand.com/*/logout.html"
    ]
  }
}

credential-type: X509_GENERATED uses a certificate-based credential instead of a client secret — recommended for production.

Both service instances are bound to the app via manifest.yml:

applications:
  - name: poc-iflow-pp-fe
    memory: 512M
    buildpacks:
      - nodejs_buildpack
    services:
      - iflow-pp-ias-svc
      - iflow-pp-destination-svc

Ensure the User's Email Matches S/4HANA Identity

The Cloud Connector Subject Pattern (configured under Cloud To On-Premise → Principal Propagation) determines how the X.509 certificate CN is built. The default pattern is:

CN=${name}

${name} is sourced from the user_name claim in the PI Runtime token. During the OAuth2JWTBearer exchange, PI Runtime populates user_name from the email claim of the incoming IAS JWT — not from sub or any other claim.

So the identity that reaches Cloud Connector and ultimately S/4HANA is always the user's email address.
This behaviour is important because many troubleshooting efforts focus on the IAS sub claim, while principal propagation in this flow ultimately depends on the user's email address being propagated and resolvable by the backend system.

Important: Make sure the user's email address in IAS matches a user identity that S/4HANA can resolve — either via direct email-to-username mapping on the S/4 side, or as the logon name if S/4 uses email as the logon name format.
Depending on your identity governance requirements, the Cloud Connector Subject Pattern can use mapping logic beyond a basic CN. On the S/4HANA side, certificate-to-user mapping (CERTRULE) adds a second layer of control, allowing a certificate CN to be mapped to a different logon name. For complex scenarios, refer to the Cloud Connector Subject Pattern Syntax and S/4HANA Certificate-Based User Mapping documentation to design patterns that fit your enterprise architecture.

To verify what identity Cloud Connector is propagating, check CC Admin UI → Logging and Tracing — the outbound request will show the CN value used in the short-lived certificate.

Here's how my configuration looks like in Cloud Connector:

 Here's how rule-based certificate mapping looks like in S/4HANA - /ncertrule

Create Process Integration Runtime Service Instance

You probably has setup this one in order to trigger any published iFlow; or if you have one but has not included below grant type, then you can create a new one in BTP Cockpit → Service Marketplace → Process Integration Runtime:

  • Plan: integration-flow
  • Grant Types to enable:
    • client_credentials (for testing)
    • jwt bearer (the one that we need)
    • refresh_token
    • saml2 bearer
  • Create a Service Key and note down clientid, clientsecret, and tokenurl

Why PI Runtime and not IAS credentials? In my landscape setup, SAP Integration Suite only trusts tokens issued by its own OAuth authorization server (via the PI Runtime instance - current the SAP Integration Suite is authenticated via SAP Default idP). IAS tokens forwarded directly are not trusted by SAP Integration Suite unless full IAS-to-Integration Suite trust federation is set up. The PI Runtime service key is the standard supported path.

Configure BTP Destination

In BTP Cockpit → Connectivity → Destinations → New Destination:

FieldValue
Name<unique destination name, i.e. m25_iflow>
TypeHTTP
URLhttps://<your-cpi-host>/http/<your-iflow-endpoint>
Proxy TypeInternet
AuthenticationOAuth2JWTBearer
Client ID<clientid from PI Runtime service key>
Client Secret<clientsecret from PI Runtime service key>
Token Service URL<tokenurl from PI Runtime service key>

No additional properties required.

Common pitfalls:

  • Do not use OAuth2UserTokenExchange — it uses refresh_token grant internally which PI Runtime does not support
  • Do not use IAS credentials here — SAP Integration Suite will reject them
  • The destination name must exactly match what is referenced in xs-app.json (watch for underscore vs hyphen)

BTP App — Approuter Setup

The frontend app is a plain HTML file served by the SAP Approuter (@sap/approuter). No custom middleware needed.

package.json:

{
  "name": "ar-iflow-pp-fe",
  "dependencies": {
    "@sap/approuter": "^22.0.3"
  },
  "scripts": {
    "start": "node node_modules/@sap/approuter/approuter.js"
  }
}

xs-app.json — routes inbound requests to the BTP destination or local static files:
Also remember to double-check the Destination name here in case you're using different name.

{
  "welcomeFile": "/index.html",
  "authenticationMethod": "route",
  "logout": { "logoutEndpoint": "/logout" },
  "routes": [
    {
      "source": "^/user-api(.*)",
      "target": "$1",
      "service": "sap-approuter-userapi"
    },
    {
      "source": "^/m25_iflow(.*)$",
      "destination": "m25_iflow",
      "csrfProtection": false,
      "target": "$1",
      "authenticationType": "ias"
    },
    {
      "source": "^/(.*)$",
      "localDir": "dist",
      "authenticationType": "ias",
      "cacheControl": "no-cache, no-store, must-revalidate"
    }
  ]
}

The /user-api route uses the built-in Approuter user info service — no custom code required to read the logged-in user's details.

dist/index.html — the entire frontend in one file:

<!DOCTYPE html>
<html lang="en">
<head>
  <meta charset="UTF-8" />
  <title>Principal Propagation PoC</title>
  <style>
    body { font-family: Arial, sans-serif; max-width: 600px; margin: 40px auto; padding: 0 16px; }
    button { padding: 8px 20px; margin-top: 8px; cursor: pointer; }
    pre { background: #f4f4f4; padding: 12px; white-space: pre-wrap; word-break: break-all; margin-top: 8px; }
  </style>
</head>
<body>

<h2>Principal Propagation PoC</h2>

<h3>Logged-in User</h3>
  <pre id="userInfo">Loading...</pre>

<h3>Trigger iFlow</h3>
  <button onclick="callIflow()">Call iFlow (GET /m25_iflow)</button>
  <pre id="iflowResponse">Response will appear here</pre>

<script>
    // Fetch logged-in user info from built-in Approuter user API
    fetch("/user-api/currentUser")
      .then(r => r.json())
      .then(u => document.getElementById("userInfo").textContent = JSON.stringify(u, null, 2))
      .catch(e => document.getElementById("userInfo").textContent = "Error: " + e.message);

// Trigger iFlow via BTP Destination (OAuth2JWTBearer)
    function callIflow() {
      call("/m25_iflow");
    }

function call(url) {
      document.getElementById("iflowResponse").textContent = "Calling " + url + "...";
      fetch(url, {
        headers: { "X-Requested-With": "XMLHttpRequest" }
      })
        .then(async r => {
          const text = await r.text();
          try { return "HTTP " + r.status + "\n\n" + JSON.stringify(JSON.parse(text), null, 2); } catch (_) { return "HTTP " + r.status + "\n\n" + text; }
        })
        .then(body => document.getElementById("iflowResponse").textContent = body)
        .catch(e => document.getElementById("iflowResponse").textContent = "Error: " + e.message);
    }
  </script>

</body>
</html>

Deploy:

cf push

Assign ESBMessaging.send Role to Users (IAS Group Mapping)

In BTP Cockpit → Security → Role Collections → Create (e.g. CPIMessagingSend) :

Roles tab — add:

Application IdentifierRole TemplateRole
it-rt-<your-instance>ESBMessagingESBMessaging.send

User Groups tab — add:

Identity ProviderName
<your-ias-tenant>.accounts.ondemand.com — Business UsersIntegration_Senders

Make sure your users are members of the Integration_Senders group in IAS, and select Business Users (not Application Users) as the user type — logged-in app users are business users in IAS.

iFlow configuration summary: The iFlow uses an HTTPS Sender Adapter with User Role authorization (ESBMessaging.send) and an OData/HTTP Receiver Adapter configured for On-Premise proxy type with Principal Propagation authentication. See the Integration Flow section below for the full configuration.

Building Integration Flow

This section assumes you are familiar with creating integration flows in Cloud Integration of SAP Integration Suite.

The integration flow in this demo scenario is intentionally minimal — its purpose is to receive the inbound call from the BTP web app, forward it to S/4HANA via Cloud Connector with Principal Propagation, and return the response.

The iFlow contains the following steps:

  1. HTTPS Sender Adapter — receives the inbound HTTP request from the BTP Approuter
  2. Request-Reply step — forwards the call to the S/4HANA OData service
  3. OData V2 Receiver Adapter — calls S/4HANA on-premise via Cloud Connector

HTTPS Sender Adapter configuration:

FieldValue
Address/your-iflow-endpoint
AuthorizationUser Role
User RoleESBMessaging.send

OData V2 Receiver Adapter — Connection tab:

FieldValue
Addresshttp://<virtual-host>:<port>/sap/opu/odata/sap/<service-name>
Proxy TypeOn-Premise
AuthenticationPrincipal Propagation
Location ID<your Cloud Connector location ID>
Custom Query Parametersap-client=<client no.>

OData V2 Receiver Adapter — Processing tab:

FieldValue
OperationQuery (GET)
Resource Path<EntitySetName>
Query Options$top=10 (or as needed)
Custom Query Options<SAP Client Number>

The sap-client parameter must go in Custom Query Parameters (Connection tab). The Resource Path must have no leading slash — e.g. `A_GLAccountInChartOfAccounts`, not `/A_GLAccountInChartOfAccounts`.

Testing the Flow

With the web app deployed, open it in the browser. You will see the logged-in user's identity displayed (via the /user-api/currentUser endpoint exposed by the Approuter). Click Call via iFlow (GET /m25_iflow) button to trigger the integration flow.

A successful end-to-end flow will show the same user email across the BTP app, the Integration Suite message log, and the S/4HANA audit log. The sections below describe how to verify each layer.

Cloud Connector — Trace

If the call reaches the Cloud Connector but something goes wrong on the S/4HANA side, enable tracing in the Cloud Connector Admin UI:

  1. Go to Cloud Connector Admin UI → Logs and Trace → Trace (Debug level)
  2. Trigger the iFlow again from the web app
  3. View the trace (scc_core.trc) and search for the CN value — this confirms which user identity Cloud Connector placed in the short-lived X.509 certificate sent to S/4HANA

This is especially useful when the call appears to succeed from the Integration Suite side but S/4HANA returns a 401 or an authorization error — it lets you confirm the CN matches a user S/4HANA can resolve.

S/4HANA — ICF Trace (`/nsectrace`)

For the deepest level of verification, enable ICF tracing on the S/4HANA side to see exactly what the system receives at the HTTP layer:

  1. In S/4HANA, run transaction /nsectrace
  2. Navigate to ICF Service / Path and enable the trace for the relevant ICF service path (e.g. `/sap/opu/odata/sap/<service-name>`) and click Activate Trace
  3. Trigger the iFlow again from the web app
  4. Back in /nsectrace, click Deactivate Trace, and choose Show Trace — the trace output will show the inbound request headers including the ssl-client-subject field, which contains the CN from the Cloud Connector certificate

The CN value here is what S/4HANA evaluates for user mapping. If it does not match a known logon name, the call will fail with a 401 even if everything upstream is correct.

Key Takeaways

Principal Propagation is more than a technical configuration—it enables auditability, compliance, and least-privilege access control by ensuring S/4HANA receives requests as the actual business user rather than a shared service account.

The implementation approach depends on customer requirements and the overall solution architecture. Whether user identity is propagated through direct connectivity or a middleware layer, the objective remains the same: maintain end-to-end user context while balancing integration needs, governance, operational considerations, and cost.

One of the key lessons learned is that principal propagation depends on several components being configured consistently across BTP, Integration Suite, Cloud Connector, and S/4HANA. Even small trust or authorization gaps can prevent user identity from being propagated successfully, making end-to-end validation essential.

References

The following SAP documentation was useful while implementing and troubleshooting this scenario.

SAP BTP - Cloud Foundry Environment

  • Administration and Operations in the Cloud Foundry

SAP Cloud Connector and S/4HANA (ABAP)

  • Cloud Connector Documentation
  • Installation Guide
  • Managing Subaccounts
  • Principal Propagation Configuration
  • Configure Access Control (HTTP)
  • Configure Subject Patterns
  • Subject Pattern Rules API
  • S/4HANA / ABAP – Rule-Based Certificate Mapping (CERTRULE)

SAP Integration Suite

  • SAP Integration Suite Documentation
  • Cloud Integration Documentation

SAP Cloud Identity Services

  • Identity Authentication (IAS) Documentation
  • Destination Service Documentation
  • OAuth2JWTBearer Authentication

entry.title: SAP BTP Account Architecture Best Practices: A Practitioner's Field Guide
entry.content:
This blog covers the architectural patterns and configuration decisions that I consistently observe separating healthy BTP landscapes from technically indebted ones. The patterns here apply across SAP BTP release versions and are validated against production landscapes in enterprise accounts.

Applies to: SAP Business Technology Platform (BTP) - all commercially available runtimes including Cloud Foundry, Kyma, and ABAP Environment. SAP Integration Suite 6.x. BTP Cockpit as of Q1 2025.

1. Global account and subaccount hierarchy design

The foundational decision in any BTP implementation is the account hierarchy. The most common architectural error is treating the Global Account as a flat container — one or two subaccounts shared across all teams, environments, and workloads.

   
Hierarchy level PurposeConfiguration scope Recommended granularity
Global AccountCommercial boundary, entitlement poolEntitlement allocation, member managementOne per enterprise (or one per legal entity)
DirectoryOrganisational grouping, policy inheritanceRole collection assignments, entitlement distributionOne per business domain or region
    
SubaccountRuntime isolation, cost unit, governance boundaryService instances, subscriptions, trust configurationOne per domain × environment (Dev/Test/Prod)

Common mistake: Sharing a single subaccount across Development, Test, and Production workloads. This creates cost attribution problems, governance risks (role assignments affecting production from dev actions), and makes regulatory compliance significantly harder. Separate subaccounts per environment are non-negotiable for enterprise-grade landscapes.

Subaccount naming convention

Establish a naming convention before your first subaccount is created. A consistent pattern:

 
Naming pattern

{company-prefix}-{domain}-{environment} Examples: acme-finance-dev acme-finance-tst acme-finance-prd acme-supplychain-dev acme-hr-prd

This naming makes cost reports, audit logs, and governance reviews immediately interpretable. It also prevents the proliferation of creatively named subaccounts that accumulates over time without a standard.

2. Integration Suite architecture patterns

SAP Integration Suite v6.x is architecturally distinct from its on-premise predecessor (SAP Process Integration / Process Orchestration). Migrating existing iFlow logic without rethinking integration patterns captures a fraction of the available value.

API-first design with API Management

API Management within Integration Suite allows teams to publish, secure, rate-limit, monetise, and analyse APIs as governed products. In most BTP landscapes I work with, this capability is underutilised — teams use Integration Suite purely for message routing while API Management remains unconfigured.

1. Design APIs before integration flows

Define the API contract (OpenAPI 3.0 specification) before building the iFlow that implements it. This enforces API-first discipline and makes the integration independently testable.

2. Publish all external-facing APIs through API Management

Even internal APIs should be governed through the API portal for discoverability, security policy enforcement, and usage analytics.

3. Configure event mesh for asynchronous patterns

SAP Event Mesh (included in Integration Suite) enables event-driven architecture patterns. Use it for high-volume, decoupled integrations instead of synchronous HTTPS calls where latency and reliability requirements allow.

4. Implement centralised error handling

Define a standard error handling iFlow pattern that all integration flows inherit. Centralise alerting via the Integration Suite Operations Monitor.

3. Entitlement planning and consumption governance

BTP's consumption-based commercial model requires active governance. Services provisioned without a framework for tracking and managing consumption produce unpredictable costs.

BTP Service Catalogue structure

Recommended catalogue fields

Service Name | BTP technical name (e.g. it-rt) Business Owner | Named individual accountable for value and cost Domain | Business domain (Finance, HR, Supply Chain...) Environment | Dev / Test / Prod Monthly Consumption | Units consumed (Cloud Credits, API calls, GB) Budget Allocated | Approved monthly spend Use Case | What business problem this service solves Review Date | Quarterly review schedule

BTP Cockpit tip: Use the Usage Analytics section under your Global Account to export monthly consumption reports per subaccount. Schedule a monthly review against your Service Catalogue. Set budget alerts in the BTP Cockpit under Global Account → Entitlements → Alerts.

4. Clean core extensibility — BTP ABAP Environment and CAP

Clean core extensibility means building all business logic extensions on BTP rather than in the S/4HANA core. Two primary extension stacks are available:

 
Extension typeTechnology  Use whenKey service
ABAP extensionsBTP ABAP Environment (Steampunk)ABAP development skills, ABAP APIs requiredabap (BTP service plan)
Application extensionsSAP Cloud Application Programming Model (CAP)New business applications, Node.js/Java skills availableCloud Foundry runtime
    
Low-code extensionsSAP Build Apps / SAP Build Process AutomationBusiness-user-driven extensions, rapid prototypingsapappstudio / process-automation
UI extensionsSAP Fiori Elements + BTPUI adaptations, custom Fiori appshtml5-apps-repo

Important: Extensions built using the released ABAP APIs (C1-released objects only) in BTP ABAP Environment are upgrade-stable. Extensions that call unreleased SAP internal APIs are not supported and will break during upgrades. Always check the API release status in the ABAP Development Tools (ADT) before using any API in an extension.

5. Developer experience and CI/CD pipeline

A BTP CI/CD pipeline established in sprint one prevents the manual deployment practices that accumulate as deployment debt over the life of a programme.

 
Recommended BTP CI/CD pipeline stages

Stage 1: Build └─ npm install / mvn package / abapbuild └─ Unit tests └─ Static code analysis (ABAP Test Cockpit / ESLint) Stage 2: Integration Test (Dev subaccount) └─ Deploy to BTP Dev └─ API integration tests └─ iFlow functional tests (if Integration Suite) Stage 3: Staged Deployment └─ Deploy to BTP Test → automated smoke tests └─ Manual approval gate └─ Deploy to BTP Prod Tools: SAP Continuous Integration and Delivery service (BTP-native) or Jenkins / GitHub Actions with BTP CLI (btp CLI v2.x)

Summary

  • Use a Directory → Subaccount hierarchy per domain × environment — never share subaccounts across environments
  • Publish all external APIs through API Management — use Event Mesh for asynchronous integration patterns
  • Maintain a BTP Service Catalogue with named owners and monthly consumption tracking
  • Use only C1-released ABAP APIs in BTP ABAP Environment extensions to ensure upgrade stability
  • Establish a CI/CD pipeline using SAP CI/CD Service or BTP CLI in sprint one

entry.title: SAP Business AI in SAP Activate: Three Dimensions of AI for Your Implementation
entry.content:
This is the first blog post in a series introducing the topic of AI in SAP Activate. Follow this blog as we will update it with links to other posts as they are released.

Last update: July 15th 2026, with links to additional related blog posts at the end of the article.

In this blog post, I want to share how SAP Activate is evolving with SAP Business AI and what is already available to you today to leverage in your implementation projects. As SAP expands the availability of AI capabilities and more assistants and agents become available for our customers, SAP Activate is keeping pace to ensure you have the guidance you need to adopt, extend, and apply these capabilities effectively during your journey to the Autonomous Suite.

When we think about Business AI in the context of SAP Activate, we organize it into three interconnected dimensions. Each dimension addresses a different aspect of how AI applies in the implementation lifecycle. In the rest of this post, I will walk you through each of these dimensions, explain what is available today, and highlight how you can start benefiting from these capabilities right away.

Three Dimensions of Business AI in SAP Activate

Before I get into the details, let me introduce the framework. The three dimensions are:

  1. Adopting Business AI capabilities and SAP Joule by SAP customers during their implementation and beyond.
  2. Extending SAP solutions with AI capabilities and agents built on the SAP Business AI Platform.
  3. Leveraging AI capabilities for implementation activities.

These dimensions are interconnected but address different audiences and project stages. Let me expand on each one.

1. Adopting SAP Business AI and SAP Joule

The first dimension is about guiding you, the customer, to activate, set up, and adopt SAP Joule and SAP Business AI capabilities as part of your implementation. In other words, this is about integrating AI into how your organization runs its business processes in the SAP Autonomous Suite.

Think of it this way: when your project team starts scoping the implementation and later runs fit-to-standard workshops, they should be considering not only the standard business processes but also the AI capabilities that come with them. Which processes benefit from AI-powered automation or recommendations? Where does SAP Joule provide value to your end users in their day-to-day work? What agents and assistants would enrich the business processes in our organization? These are the questions SAP Activate helps you answer.

Already in 2025, SAP Activate was enhanced with guidance to help customers activate, set up, and adopt SAP Joule and available SAP Business AI capabilities (see the Business AI catalog on SAP Discovery Center). The guidance in SAP Activate includes self-enablement tasks, guidance for running fit-to-standard workshops with AI considerations, and procedures for setting up the necessary integrations. As an example, the information for the setup of SAP Joule in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition is available here (the tasks are tagged with Joule tag).

How do you find this content? You can select the SAP Business AI and SAP Joule tags in the road map viewer in SAP for Me to identify the relevant tasks for your implementation scenario. The flow is straightforward: identify the available SAP Business AI capabilities and SAP Joule features, assess and validate them during fit-to-standard, activate and set up during Realize, and enable your business users to adopt the new capability.

Example of a task for the initial setup of Joule in SAP Cloud ERP Private

We plan to continue expanding this coverage throughout 2026, with increased focus on the new AI capabilities and agents as they become available to customers, and to incorporate steps to consider and embed these capabilities into the scope of your implementation.

Dimension 2: Extending with SAP Business AI Platform Services

The second dimension is for organizations that need to go beyond the pre-built AI capabilities. It supports customers in leveraging the SAP Business AI Platform to build custom AI solutions and agents that extend standard SAP application capabilities in line with the clean core strategy.

This is an important distinction: the first dimension is about adopting what SAP delivers out of the box. The second dimension is about building your own AI-powered extensions on the platform when your business requires something unique. The key principle here is that these extensions follow the clean core approach, ensuring your solution remains upgradeable and maintainable.

SAP Activate for SAP Business Technology Platform already covers various AI-related topics, including guidance for application development on SAP BTP and implementation guidance for AI capabilities. The use of platform services to extend solution guidance is also embedded in the implementation roadmaps for our ERP solutions in the Extensibility work stream, for both private and public cloud solutions. You can access it here.

The flow in your project follows the extensibility work stream: identify and confirm extensibility requirements during fit-to-standard workshops (or earlier if the requirements are already known), leverage SAP Business AI Platform services to extend the standard solution and test during the Realize phase, and finally enable your business users to adopt the new capability.

We plan to continue evolving this implementation guidance as new platform capabilities and services become available, including the new agents and assistants announced at SAPPHIRE 2026.

Dimension 3: Leveraging AI Capabilities for Implementation

The third dimension is where I expect many of you reading this post will find the most immediate value. This is about applying AI capabilities throughout your implementation project to accelerate requirements gathering, design, development, testing, and knowledge transfer across the project lifecycle.

This dimension covers a broad range of AI capabilities like Joule on road map viewer, SAP Joule for Consultants, and embedded AI capabilities in the integrated toolchain, including SAP LeanIX, SAP Signavio, SAP Cloud ALM, and WalkMe.

AI-Assisted Tasks

Earlier in June, SAP Activate released the first wave of AI-assisted implementation tasks. This initial release included 9 updated SAP Activate tasks, 13 new tested prompts, and updated accelerators, focused on SAP Activate for SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition and SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition.

These AI-assisted tasks are designed to support your project team in leveraging AI capabilities during implementation. Rather than focusing on information retrieval, we focused on high-impact tasks and deliverables where AI can make a real difference. SAP Activate delivers tested prompts and example outputs, and in some cases, updated accelerators.

The initial focus is on Joule for Consultants, SAP Cloud ALM, and SAP Test Automation Tool through all project stages, covering seven front-runner topics:

  1. Self-enablement on Joule for Consultants – helping your team get started with the capability.
  2. Project execution and artifacts preparation – accelerating the creation of project management deliverables.
  3. Preparation for fit-to-standard workshops – using AI to help consultants prepare effectively for these critical sessions.
  4. Generating requirements from fit-to-standard workshop artifacts – processing workshop results into requirements and user stories in SAP Cloud ALM with assistance from Joule for Consultants.
  5. Generating design documents for extensions – using AI to draft extension design documentation.
  6. Preparing for data loads  – leveraging AI to help with data migration strategy and approach.
  7. Testing – preparing testing strategy and approach, as well as automating preparation of test scripts, determination of test scope, and management of testing.

An AI-assisted task includes two key elements: an AI-assisted procedure section in the task description that explains how to use the AI capability, and a link to the prompt library. The prompt library provides access to the prompt text, example output, and additional details for prompting the AI tool. We built a centralized prompt library to maintain the integrity of prompts linked to SAP Activate and to ensure all users can access them, even if they do not yet have direct access to the AI capability where a dedicated prompt library may exist.

You can find these tasks by filtering for the AI-Assisted Procedure tag in the SAP Activate roadmap viewer or SAP Cloud ALM when accessing the Task Management app. The SAP Activate team will continue to expand the coverage with AI-assisted tasks in the future. Let us know your feedback on the current tasks and your suggestions for areas to enhance.

Joule in SAP Activate Road Map Viewer on SAP for Me

I also want to highlight a capability that has been available since June 2025 and continues to bring great value to our users. SAP Joule is embedded directly in the SAP Activate road map viewer on SAP for Me. This is a conversational AI assistant that enables you to surface the guidance you need through natural-language questions, eliminating the need to manually navigate the roadmap structures.

You can ask Joule your SAP Activate-related questions, carry out a conversation, and copy the answers to share with your partners or colleagues. For example, you might ask about the sequence of activities for data migration, or about which accelerators are available for a specific topic. Joule will provide a clear response based on the SAP Activate content.

To try it, navigate to the SAP Activate road map viewer and click on the Joule icon in the top right corner. You can read more about this capability in the blog post from Chandan: Say Hi to Joule in SAP Activate road map viewer.

Outlook

Looking ahead, we will continue enriching SAP Activate with AI guidance for implementation experts – project managers, consultants, developers, and architects – on how to use available AI capabilities during projects.

In the mid-to-long term, we will evolve SAP Activate as new agents and assistants become available in SAP Cloud ALM, aligned with the agentic AI transformation platform vision shared at SAPPHIRE 2026. We anticipate significant evolution as new agents are built and integrated into the offerings through 2026.

I encourage you to explore the AI-assisted tasks in the road map viewer, try Joule on SAP for Me, and share your experience. Please use the comments to this blog post to continue the conversation or reach out to us at sapactivatemethodology@sap.com with your feedback and suggestions. We are excited to hear how you are leveraging these capabilities in your projects.

Other blogs in this series

To deep-dive into AI-assisted tasks and capabilities, access the blogs linked below (we will add the links as the blogs get published).

  • Introducing the SAP Activate Prompt Library: Curated AI Prompts for Your Implementation Project by Chandan Gurav
  • AI-Assisted Extensibility Planning & Design in SAP Activate for SAP S/4HANA Cloud by Kiran Bose
  • Work Smarter with AI-Assisted Tasks in the SAP Activate Project Management Workstream by Christina Galbreath
  • SAP Activate, AI and Data Management by Daniel Ciecko
  • Transform Your Fit-to-Standard Workshop Preparation with AI-Assisted Procedures by Jair Azevedo

Useful Resources

  • SAP Activate road map viewer
  • Introducing Joule
  • Setting Up Joule Across Your Organizational System Landscapes
  • Introducing SAP Joule for Consultants
  • Implementing Joule Across Your Organizational System Landscape

entry.title: Joule for Developers
entry.content:
Joule's ABAP developer capabilities support full-stack development in ABAP Cloud. It provides real-time explanations of development objects and is fully integrated in the ABAP Development Tools for Eclipse, offering accurate information about open objects or marked code. Joule predicts and generates subsequent lines of code based on ABAP Doc, comments, or code being typed, taking the ABAP language version into account to suggest fitting code. It can also generate end-to-end ABAP cloud development scenarios including all required objects.

SAP Joule for developers, ABAP AI capabilities is designed to support you with your day-to-day tasks when working with ABAP code, to increase your efficiency and to lower total cost of development.  In the SAP ecosystem specifically across SAP BTP (ABAP Environment) and SAP S/4HANA Cloud - Joule matters because it understands the proprietary context of ABAP Cloud, Core Data Services (CDS), and SAP Fiori.

You can explore and understand in detail extensively regarding the capabilities of Joule by referring to the blog - Joule Capabilities for Developers ABAP - SAP Community but this blog details on how you can get onboard and capitalise this AI developer journey.

How to get started?

This AI feature is available as a free of charge Promo until October 2026 after which it will be a Premium AI feature. 

Required Parent SKUs (The Foundation)

To activate the Joule for Developers add-on, your global account must have the underlying infrastructure provisioned. 
Customer SKU  : 8019164
Partner SKU      : 8019670

 

 

This promotional license for Joule for Developers  was sunset as of June 1,2026 . The SAP Note 3571857 for the SAP BTP ABAP environment and SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition and it  described the activation and enablement (commercial and technical) of the promotional license for SAP Joule for Developers, ABAP AI capabilities. No new orders can be placed. Existing contracts will be fulfilled until end of September 2026 at the maximum.

A new commercial model for SAP Joule for Developers, ABAP AI capabilities was introduced, based on AI units, see SAP note https://me.sap.com/notes/3740750. 


Sharing our latest blog on SAP Joule for Developers , ABAP AI capabilities, now available through a new AI Unit-based commercial license.
The blog covers:
• Licensing and commercial aspects
• Backend configuration and enablement steps and SAP Joule for Developers, ABAP AI Capabilities in ABAP Development Tools for  Eclipse and Visual Studio Code
🔗SAP Joule for Developers, ABAP AI capabilities inc... - SAP Community

The capabilities of Joule for Developers are much more extensive than just basic code generation. Here is a comprehensively updated, tabularized "Feature Deep Dive" based strictly on the extracted official capabilities:

Feature

What it does

How it works technically / Examples

Joule Chat

Acts as an AI pair programmer that you can interact with using natural language for development tasks.

Uses IDE Context Awareness to read currently opened resources. It answers general ABAP questions, explains specific code snippets, and dynamically generates formatted code examples you can directly insert.

Predictive Code Completion

Automatically generates inline "ghost text" to predict and complete code as you type.

Listens to cursor position and editor context. It supports ABAP interfaces, classes, and programs. It is powered by a fine-tuned model (Star Coder 2) trained specifically on SAP internal ABAP source code.

Explain Capability

Provides detailed, semantic explanations of selected development objects or lines of source code.

Fully integrated into ADT. You can select an ABAP class, a CDS artifact, or lines of code, and Joule provides an English explanation. It also supports explaining Custom Code Migration (ATC) findings directly in-app.

Documentation Chat

Allows you to chat directly with public documentation hosted on the SAP Help Portal.

Accesses standard guides (ABAP Keyword Documentation, RAP, ABAP Data Models, ABAP Cloud) and provides referenced answers considering your current project context.

ABAP Unit Test Generation

Automatically creates and refactors ABAP Unit tests to check functional correctness.

Generates tests for public/protected/private methods. It also performs Dependency Analysis to find test-unfriendly dependencies, automatically adds Test Doubles, and helps split or refactor large test classes.

CDS Unit Test Generation

Generates automated test classes and context-specific test data for ABAP CDS entities.

Provides basic test coverage by reading the semantics of a CDS entity and using AI to generate test method names, test data, and updatable test doubles.

OData UI Service from Scratch

Generates a complete RAP BO (Business Object) and UI service from an empty package.

You prompt Joule with requirements (e.g., "Create a Travel BO with two entities"). It automatically generates the database tables, CDS views, behavior definitions, metadata extensions, and service bindings.

RAP Business Logic Prediction

Generates complex implementation logic for RAP determinations and validations.

Triggered via a quick fix inside an empty method. You provide a natural language description (e.g., what to validate, error messages, edge cases), and Joule generates the exact ABAP code for the behavior pool.

Consume Capability

Auto-generates OData requests and the corresponding ABAP code to consume remote services.

You prompt Joule (e.g., "I want to read the field ID from all campaigns"). It outputs the HTTP GET request and the specific ABAP logic required to use the OData Client Proxy.

Embedded Analytics Star Schema Generator

Uses AI to derive and create an analytical star schema based on existing CDS view entities.

Automatically generates the Cube definition (center of the star schema), dimension fields (e.g., Product, Region), and measure fields required for complex ABAP analytics.

GenAI-Driven Extensibility Assistant

Assists developers in executing various tasks related to field extensibility.

Helps find business contexts, usages, value help views, and enables business scenarios when creating custom fields for extensibility.

Custom Code Migration AI Assistant

Accelerates the migration of legacy custom code to modern, clean-core standards.

Integrates with the "Analyze Custom Code / Custom Code Migration" Fiori app to provide deep AI explanations of ATC (ABAP Test Cockpit) simplification findings and legacy processing logic.


Stay tuned as many more features are soon rolling out.  

References:

  • SAP Discovery Center AI Feature - SAP BTP, ABAP environment, SAP Joule for Developers, ABAP AI capabilities
  • SAP Joule for Developers, ABAP AI Capabilities | SAP Help Portal










References:

  • SAP Discovery Center AI Feature - SAP BTP, ABAP environment, SAP Joule for Developers, ABAP AI capabilities
  • SAP Joule for Developers, ABAP AI Capabilities | SAP Help Portal

entry.title: SAP Business AI Platform: What It Means for SAP BTP & SAP BDC Customers – Live Expert Session
entry.content:
At SAP Sapphire 2026, SAP introduced one of its most significant platform announcements in recent years: the SAP Business AI Platform (BAIP). By bringing together SAP Business Technology Platform (BTP), SAP Business Data Cloud (BDC), and SAP Business Transformation Management (BTM), SAP has outlined a vision for how enterprises can build, govern, and scale AI-driven business processes across their landscapes.

While the announcement generated substantial interest, it has also raised an important question within the SAP community:

What does this actually mean for existing SAP BTP and SAP Business Data Cloud customers?

 

For many architects, developers, platform owners, data professionals, and SAP user group members, the strategic direction is exciting—but the practical implications are less clear.

  • Is BAIP primarily a rebranding exercise or a significant evolution of SAP's platform strategy?
  • How will BTP fit into the new landscape?
  • What role does Business Data Cloud play in enabling AI?
  • Will customers need to change their current investments, architectures, or roadmaps?
  • What does this mean for day-to-day operations and technical teams?

These are exactly the questions the SAP community is asking—and exactly the questions we aim to answer.

Bringing Clarity from the Experts

To help SAP customers navigate this important announcement, I have organized a special live community session featuring leading SAP experts who are directly involved in shaping the future of these technologies.

Live Session

SAP Business AI Platform: What It Means for SAP BTP & SAP BDC Customers

📅Date: July 29, 2026
🕓Time: 16:00

What We Will Cover

During this session, we will revisit the strategic vision behind the SAP Business AI Platform and discuss how SAP intends to unify platform, data, and transformation capabilities to make agentic AI a practical reality for customers and partners.

The discussion will focus specifically on what BAIP means for:

  • SAP BTP customers
  • SAP Business Data Cloud customers
  • Enterprise architects
  • Application developers
  • Data and analytics professionals
  • Platform operations teams
  • SAP transformation leaders

Our experts will examine both the strategic and operational dimensions of the announcement:

Strategic Perspective

  • The vision behind SAP Business AI Platform
  • How BAIP reshapes the SAP platform landscape
  • The relationship between BTP, BDC, and BTM
  • Implications for customer investment strategies
  • The role of business AI in SAP's future roadmap

Practitioner Perspective

  • Impacts on platform architecture and governance
  • What developers and architects need to know
  • How data foundations evolve with Business Data Cloud
  • Practical considerations for current SAP BTP and SAP BDC users

Meet the Experts

We are fortunate to welcome an exceptional panel of SAP thought leaders:

  • Steffen Schad – Head of Outbound Product Management, SAP BTP
  • KP Sauer – Senior Director Data Warehousing, SAP
  • Dan Kearnan – Senior Director, SAP BTP

This is a unique opportunity to hear directly from experts who can provide both strategic context and practical guidance on how SAP's latest platform direction affects customers today and in the future.

Join the Conversation

SAP user groups have always played a critical role in helping customers understand major technology transitions. With the introduction of the SAP Business AI Platform, many organizations are evaluating how their existing BTP and Business Data Cloud investments fit into the bigger picture.

My goal with this session is simple: to bring clarity to the community and help SAP professionals understand what SAP BAIP means beyond the headlines.

Whether you are responsible for platform strategy, architecture, development, analytics, or data management, this discussion will provide valuable insights into SAP's evolving AI-first platform vision.

Register Now

🔗Registration:
https://events.sap.com/baip/en_us/home.html

Watch Live on YouTube

📺Live Stream:
https://youtube.com/live/6r2MNgbrT4Q

Add to Your Calendar

📅Calendar Link:
https://community.sap.com/khhcw49343/downloadCalendar?message.id=14441627 

The SAP community thrives on open discussions and shared learning. I hope you'll join us on July 29 as we explore one of the most important SAP platform announcements of 2026 and gain direct insight into what it means for BTP and Business Data Cloud customers moving forward. We look forward to seeing you there.

Kind regards

Tania

Global User Groups Organization

entry.title: SAP Event Mesh for Beginners – Part 3: Push Delivery with Webhooks
entry.content:
SAP Event Mesh for Beginners – Part 3: Push Delivery with Webhooks

Part 3 of the SAP Event Mesh beginner series.

A quick recap

By the end of Part 2, you had events publishing into Event Mesh over HTTP and CPI consuming them from a queue.

Part 1 (this post):  EDA concepts + provisioning the Event Mesh instance, queues, subscriptions, and service key (https://community.sap.com/t5/technology-blog-posts-by-members/sap-event-mesh-for-beginners-part-1-event-driven-architecture-topics-queues/ba-p/14428493)

Part 2: Testing with Postman + CPI iFlow configuration (publish & consume) (https://community.sap.com/t5/technology-blog-posts-by-members/sap-event-mesh-for-beginners-part-2-testing-with-postman-amp-cpi-iflow/ba-p/14428855)

Part 3: Push Delivery with Webhooks 
Part 4:  Common errors, fixes, and a quick-reference cheat sheet

But there's a catch with everything we've done so far. Every consumption approach in Part 2 was pull-based — your CPI iFlow (AMQP Sender, or the REST /consumption call) reaches out to the queue and asks "any messages for me?" That works, but it means CPI is doing the polling, and there's always a small delay.

There's a second delivery model that flips this around: the webhook. Instead of CPI pulling, Event Mesh pushes — the moment a message lands in the queue, Event Mesh calls your endpoint. No polling, near real-time, and it's the pattern most S/4HANA → CPI integrations actually use in the wild.

If you went looking for "webhook" on the Queue screen and couldn't find it — you're not missing anything. It lives somewhere else, and we'll get there.

 

1. Pull vs Push — when to use which

Both models start the same way: a queue subscribed to your topics collects messages. The difference is who initiates delivery.

  Pull (Part 2) Push (this post)
Who initiatesCPI reaches out to the queueEvent Mesh calls your endpoint
CPI adapterAMQP Sender / REST consumptionHTTPS Sender (receives the call)
TimingPolling intervalNear real-time
Best forBatch-style / CPI-driven pacingEvent-driven, low-latency S/4HANA → CPI

A webhook subscription connects your webhook to a queue, and when messages reach that queue, it delivers them to your webhook in near real time. Because there's no polling, it consumes fewer resources on both sides.

For most "react to an S/4HANA business event" scenarios, push (webhook) is the right choice.

2. Build the receiving iFlow first

A webhook needs somewhere to push to — an HTTPS endpoint that's ready and waiting. In CPI, that's an iFlow with an HTTP Sender adapter. Its address becomes your webhook URL.

  1. Create a new iFlow (e.g. EventMesh_Webhook_Receiver).
  2. On the Sender → HTTPS adapter:
    • Address: something like /eventmesh-webhook → the full URL becomes https://<your-cpi-tenant>/http/eventmesh-webhook
    • Authorization: User Role, role ESBMessaging.send (this is what the webhook's OAuth token will need permission for).
  3. Add whatever processing you need after the sender (map the CloudEvent, call an OData API, etc.).
  4. Deploy the iFlow — the endpoint must be live before you create the webhook, or the handshake (next section) will fail.

 

✅Tip: Copy the deployed endpoint URL from Monitor → Manage Integration Content → your iFlow → Endpoints. That exact URL is what you'll paste as the webhook URL.

3. Get the credential that lets the webhook call into CPI

Here's the single most important thing to understand about webhooks, and where almost everyone gets confused:

⚠️The webhook authenticates INTO CPI — so it uses CPI's inbound credential, not the Event Mesh service key.

Everything up to now used the Event Mesh key (so CPI could talk to Event Mesh). A webhook is the opposite direction: Event Mesh is now the caller, and it needs credentials to get into your CPI endpoint.

Those credentials come from a Process Integration Runtime service instance:

  1. In your CPI subaccount → Instances and Subscriptions → Create → service Process Integration Runtime, plan integration-flow.
  2. Add the role/grant for ESBMessaging.send (API access).
  3. Create a Service Key on it → note the clientid, clientsecret, and tokenurl.

These three values are what secure the webhook. Keep them separate in your head from the Event Mesh key — mixing them up is the #1 webhook failure.

4. Create the webhook

Now the part you couldn't find. It's not on the Queue screen — it's on the message client.

  1. In Event Mesh, open the Overview tab and click your Message Client → this opens the Webhooksconfiguration screen → click Create.
  2. Fill in:
Field Value
Namewebhook-3pl (any name)
Queue NameS4HC/Testing/Masterdata/3pl (the queue to attach to)
Webhook URLhttps://<your-cpi-tenant>/http/eventmesh-webhook (the deployed HTTPS Sender endpoint)
Quality of Service1 (recommended — see section 6)
AuthenticationOAuth2 Client Credentials
Client ID / Secret / Token URLfrom the Process Integration Runtime service key (section 3)
  1. Save. On success you'll see the subscription added.

 

From here, any message that lands in the 3pl queue is pushed to your iFlow — no polling, no AMQP Sender needed.

5. The handshake (what it is, why it fails)

Before Event Mesh sends a single real message, it does a handshake to make sure your endpoint actually agreed to receive pushes.

The handshake is an OPTIONS call to your webhook URL carrying a WebHook-Request-Origin header. Your endpoint must reply with a WebHook-Allowed-Origin header whose value matches that origin (or is *). Only after a successful handshake does Event Mesh start publishing messages to the webhook.

The good news: a properly deployed CPI HTTPS Sender endpoint handles this automatically. The usual reasons a handshake fails:

  • The iFlow isn't deployed or the endpoint isn't reachable.
  • The credentials are wrong (remember: CPI runtime key, not Event Mesh key).
  • The endpoint requires an auth the webhook isn't sending.

If the webhook shows as not active or "handshake failed," check those three before anything else.

6. QoS and the retry behavior

When you create the webhook you pick a Quality of Service:

  • QoS 0 (at most once): Event Mesh posts the message to your webhook and deletes it from the queue regardlessof whether your endpoint succeeded. Fast, but you can lose messages if CPI was down.
  • QoS 1 (at least once): Event Mesh posts the message and waits for a 2XX response as acknowledgment. Any 2XX = success, message removed. Anything else = the message stays and gets retried.

That retry behavior is worth knowing: on a non-2XX response the service resends roughly every 15 minutes, gradually slowing the frequency, and keeps redelivering for up to 30 days unless your webhook positively acknowledges. So a broken downstream won't silently drop events — but it also means a misbehaving endpoint can pile up retries.

✅Tip: For anything you can't afford to lose (master data, business events), use QoS 1. You can pause a webhook during maintenance and resume it later without losing queued messages.

7. Troubleshooting

Symptom Likely cause Fix
Handshake failed / webhook not activeiFlow not deployed, or endpoint unreachableDeploy the iFlow; confirm the endpoint URL from Monitor → Endpoints
401 on deliveryWrong credential direction — used the Event Mesh keyUse the Process Integration Runtime client id/secret/token URL
403 on deliveryOAuth client lacks the API roleGrant ESBMessaging.send to the CPI runtime instance
Messages pile up in the queue, never deliveredEndpoint returns non-2XX (QoS 1 keeps retrying)Fix the receiving iFlow so it returns 2XX; check CPI monitoring
Nothing arrives at allQueue isn't subscribed to the right topic (case-sensitive!)Re-check the queue's topic subscription — same gotcha as the rest of the series

8. Quick Reference

🔒Replace all placeholders with your own values. Never publish real secrets.

Item Value
Where to createEvent Mesh → Overview → Message Client → Webhooks → Create
Webhook URLhttps://<your-cpi-tenant>/http/<iflow-address> (deployed HTTPS Sender endpoint)
Auth typeOAuth2 Client Credentials
Auth sourceProcess Integration Runtime service key (NOT the Event Mesh key)
Required CPI roleESBMessaging.send
HandshakeOPTIONS + WebHook-Request-Origin → reply WebHook-Allowed-Origin (match or *)
QoS 0delete after post, regardless of result
QoS 1needs 2XX ack; retries ~15 min, backs off, up to 30 days
Maintenancepause / resume the webhook

What's next

Webhooks complete the delivery picture: Part 2 gave you the pull model (CPI reaches out), and this post gives you the push model (Event Mesh reaches in). For real-time S/4HANA → CPI event handling, push is usually what you want.

The one idea to carry away: credential direction. Publishing/consuming uses the Event Mesh key; the webhook uses your CPI runtime key, because the caller and callee have swapped.

In Part 4 — Troubleshooting & Quick Reference, we'll bring the whole series together: every error you're likely to hit across setup, publishing, consuming, and webhooks — with causes, fixes, and a one-page cheat sheet to keep open while you build.

🔒Security note: All client IDs, secrets, tenant URLs, and endpoints in this post are placeholders. Use your own values, keep them out of public posts, and rotate anything that's been exposed.

Primary Tag: SAP Integration Suite Tags: SAP Event Mesh, Cloud Integration, SAP CPI, Webhooks, OAuth2, Event-Driven Architecture, SAP BTP

entry.title: Partner Learning Accelerator: SAP Certified - Backend Developer - SAP CAP
entry.content:
Preparing for a certification is more than passing an exam. It's about building confidence to apply your skills when customers need them most.

If you're working with SAP BTP and developing cloud-native extensions, the Partner Learning Accelerator: SAP Certified - Backend Developer - SAP Cloud Application Programming Model is built for you.

During this 3-hour live session, you'll review the certification topics that matter most. From modeling data with CDS and building CAP applications in Node.js or Java to integrating SAP services, implementing security, and creating Fiori elements-based UIs, you'll strengthen the knowledge that supports real customer projects.

Plus, you'll hear practical guidance that helps connect certification concepts with everyday development scenarios. That's often the difference between recognizing an answer and understanding why it's the right one.

entry.title: Clean Core Levels A–D: how to classify your custom ABAP — and what to do with each grade
entry.content:
Disclosure: I'm the author of this post and the creator of Clean-Core.io, a free, non-commercial community tool I mention once near the end. This post is educational; the tool is optional.

Overview

With the 2025 update to the ABAP extensibility guide, SAP evolved the familiar 3-tier extensibility model into the clean core level concept — a four-grade classification of custom extensions: A, B, C, D. Instead of a blunt "clean vs. not clean", it tells you how closely each extension aligns with Clean Core, and it maps directly to ABAP Test Cockpit (ATC) behaviour:

 

Level Usage of SAP objects in custom development ATC behaviour

AReleased SAP APIs (local & remote) and extension pointsNo message
BClassic SAP APIs and extension points (SAP-recommended)Priority 3 · info
CInternal SAP APIs (conditionally clean via the changelog-for-SAP-objects check)Priority 2 · warning
DNot-recommended objects & extension technologies (modifications, implicit enhancements)Priority 1 · error

Level A is the cleanest — on-stack ABAP Cloud or side-by-side on SAP BTP. Levels B–D describe classic ABAP, from SAP-recommended (B) down to not-recommended (D).

Why this matters now

Every custom object you carry into S/4HANA has to be defensible. The move from a binary "clean/not clean" to A–D is a gift — it lets you transform at your own pace, prioritising the worst first instead of trying to reach "100% clean" overnight.

The real problem

Knowing the model is easy. Grading thousands of custom objects against it is not. ATC flags readiness object-by-object, and the Cloudification Repository tells you which standard objects are released — but turning that into a per-object A/B/C/D verdict plus a remediation path, across a grown Z-landscape, is still mostly manual and inconsistent between teams.

A practical classification flow

  1. Identify the custom object and what it touches (tables, APIs, function modules, enhancements).
  2. Look up each dependency: released API/extension point → A; classic-but-official API → B; internal SAP object → C; modification / not-recommended access → D.
  3. Decide remediation per grade: A keep · B keep, plan to lift later · C verify via the changelog-for-SAP-objects approach and wrap behind a released interface · D replace before the upgrade.

A worked example

A classic Z_CUSTOMER_REPORT:

SELECT kunnr, name1 FROM kna1 INTO TABLE @lt_cust.   " direct read of an internal standard table

Classify: direct access to KNA1 (an internal SAP object, not a released API) → Level C (ATC: warning). A direct write would be Level D (ATC: error).

Remediate: map to the released CDS view I_Customer → the same logic becomes Level A (ATC: no message):

SELECT Customer, CustomerName FROM I_Customer INTO TABLE @lt_cust.

One dependency, one grade, one clear next step. Repeat across the landscape and you get a prioritised backlog: fix D first (upgrade blockers), lift C, schedule B, keep A.

How I approached tooling this (optional)

To avoid doing this by hand, I built Clean-Core.io — a free, non-commercial community tool. It runs a deterministic evidence engine first (parsing the code, mapping dependencies against the catalog), then AI on top, and derives an A–D grade per detected object — aligned to the clean core level concept and its ATC priorities. It is complementary to ATC/ADT, not a replacement, and every output is a draft for a qualified architect to review. Free: clean-core.io (link in the comments).

Conclusion

The move from 3-tier to A–D isn't a relabel — it's a better mental model for prioritising custom-code remediation. Give each dependency an explicit grade, start with your Level D objects, and turn "we have a lot of Z-code" into a defensible, upgrade-ready plan.

How are you classifying your custom code against the new A–D levels today — by hand, ATC, or something else? I'd genuinely like to compare approaches in the comments.
