entry.title: Setting Up Expense Master Data in Commodity Expense Management
entry.content:
A Practical Guide for Business Users and Solution Designers

In Commodity Expense Management, setting up expense master data is not just a configuration activity—it’s a foundation for automation, accuracy, and financial transparency.

This blog walks through the structured approach to configuring expense master data, helping you understand how different applications work together to drive:

  • Intelligent expense determination
  • Automated accruals
  • Seamless financial postings

 

🧭 The Big Picture

For clarity and maintainability, expense master data is organized into four layers:

 

Basic

→             Define core expense objects

Foundation

→             Enable logic via fields & lookups

Mapping

→             Drive automation rules

Postings

→             Integrate with financial postings

Each layer builds on the previous one—so getting the design right upfront is critical.

 

1️⃣   Basic: Defining the Core Building Blocks

This is where you establish the what of expense management.

🔹Manage Expense Types

  • Logical grouping (e.g., Brokerage)
  • Enables business-semantic reporting
  • Supports multi-language descriptions
  • Where-used visibility

Lifecycle control:

  • An expense type can be de-activated to prevent any further usage if there are no active expense IDs.
  • An expense type can be deleted from the system if there are no expense IDs.

🔹Manage Expense IDs

Represents the actual expense.

Key design elements:

  • Mandatory link to Expense Type
  • Multi-language support
  • Where-used visibility

Lifecycle control:

  • An expense id can be de-activated to prevent any further usage if there are no active templates.
  • An expense id can be deleted from the system if there are no templates and transactional documents using it.

📌Firmed At vs Accrual Event

These two settings define when an expense becomes final and when it is financially recognized.

Firmed At

This setting defines when expense details (rate, service provider, payment terms, etc.) become final and unchangeable.

Options include:

  • Contract/Deal – Editable in Contract (Agri) / Deal (non-Agri), not beyond
  • Nomination – Editable up to Nomination stage
  • “-” – No restriction; editable across all documents

Accrual Event

This determines when an expense is considered incurred and should be accrued.

  • Contract Approved / Deal Published
  • Nomination Confirmed
  • Delivery Executed

👉Important Rule: Accrual Event ≥ Firmed At

🔹Manage Templates

Templates enable automation and defaulting of expenses during document creation (Contract or Deal).

A Template:

  • Contains a predefined list of expenses
  • Displays Expense Types, Firmed At, and Accrual Event indicators
  • Provides where-used visibility (via Lookups)

Lifecycle controls:

  • Deactivation (if not used in active lookups)
  • Deletion (if unused)

 

2️⃣   Foundation: Enabling Intelligent Logic

This layer defines the how behind determination.

🔹Manage Field Catalogs

Field Catalog define the input parameters used in Lookup Definitions.

Two field sources:

  • System – Predefined standard fields
  • User – Custom fields (namespace starting with Z)

Capabilities include:

  • Multi-language labels
  • Valid value definitions
  • Where-used tracking across Lookups
  • Fields can only be deleted if they are not referenced in any lookups

🔹Manage Look Up Definition

This is the most critical configuration step, as it governs how templates and expense details are determined. Two types of Lookups:

Template Determination

  • Identifies which Template to use
  • First field: Company Code (fixed)
  • Additional fields: configurable (System/User)

Expense Determination

  • Determines expense details (Service Provider, Rate, etc.)
  • First fields: Company Code and Expense ID (fixed)
  • Additional fields: configurable (System/User)

⚠️Once data is maintained → structure is locked

 

3️⃣  Mapping: Driving Automation

The Mapping layer operationalises the rules defined earlier to enable real-time determination.

🔹Manage Look Up Assignment

This step links Company Code → Lookup Definitions.

Key points:

  • One Template Lookup and one Expense Lookup per Company Code
  • Lookups can be reused across Company Codes
  • Missing assignment = no automation (manual entry of expenses required)

🔹Manage Template Determination

This app defines which Template gets selected based on input criteria.

Key characteristics:

  • Data maintained per Company Code
  • Dynamic structure based on Lookup Definition
  • Output field: Template ID
  • Allowed values: Values as defined in field Catalog and  “*” Any value

Now let us understand what this means with an example below

 

Commodity

Document Type

Incoterms

Template ID

SOYBEANS

3P Purchase

FOB

Template_SB_3P_FOB

SOYBEANS

3P Purchase

DDP

Template_SB_3P_DDP

SOYBEANS

3P Purchase

*

Template_SB_3P_ALL

So, when a contract (Agri) in this case is created the system looks out for exact match and if found then use the template for determining the list of expenses. If an exact match is not found, then the system looks out for a record by using generic search (from right to left) till it could determine a match to use the template for determining the list of expenses. If no match is found, then this results into no determination of expenses and therefore nothing is defaulted in contract.

🔹Manage Expense Determination

This defines how each Expense ID is populated with additional details such as:

  • Service Provider
  • Calculation Type
  • Rate / Amount
  • Currency, Units, UoM

Key capabilities:

  • Multi-provider support
  • Import/Export (Merge or Replace)
  • Upload logs with status tracking

Matching logic mirrors Template Determination:

  • Exact match → Use details
  • Generic match → Fallback logic
  • No match → Expense defaults without details

Below is a visual representation of how the top 3 layers stack up

 

4️⃣ Postings: Financial Integration

The Postings layer finalizes the setup by linking expenses to financial postings. This solution uses the Agency Business component for accruals and settlements.

🔹Manage Posting Assignments

Maps Expense ID to:

  • Pricing Condition Type
  • Accrual Document Type
  • Settlement Document Type
  • Offset Document Types

🔁Accrual & Settlement Behaviour

A key concept in Commodity Expense Management:

👉The system does NOT cancel postings
👉It creates offset entries

Accrual Posting (VBD)
                 ↓
Offset Accrual (Credit Memo)

Settlement Posting
                 ↓
Offset Settlement (Credit Memo)

⚠️Design Guideline:

  • Offset types must mirror original types
  • Must use Credit Memo category

 

🧠   Key Takeaways

  • Basic → Defines structure
  • Foundation → Enables logic
  • Mapping → Drives automation
  • Postings → Ensures financial accuracy

 

🚀   Final Thought

A well-designed expense master data setup enables:

✔️     Accurate planning

✔️     Intelligent automation

✔️     Real-time accruals

✔️     Clean financial postings

 

But more importantly—it creates a scalable and adaptable framework that can evolve with business complexity.

entry.title: Adding an inbuilt Translation view in C4C using SAP Translation Hub service
entry.content:
Introduction

In multilingual business environments, service agents frequently receive customer emails written in languages other than their own. For example, a German‑speaking customer service representative may receive inquiries in French or Swedish. To understand the customer’s issue and provide an appropriate response, agents often rely on external translation tools. This requires translating the incoming email, taking action based on its content, drafting a reply in their own language, and then translating the response back into the customer’s preferred language.
This multi‑step process is time‑consuming, disrupts workflow, and negatively impacts overall user experience and productivity.

 

To streamline this process and enhance user efficiency, we aim to provide an integrated translation capability directly within SAP Cloud for Customer (C4C). By leveraging the SAP Translation Hub service available on SAP Business Technology Platform (BTP), users will be able to translate incoming and outgoing messages without navigating to external tools.
This built-in solution will significantly reduce manual effort, improve turnaround time, and deliver a seamless user experience for agents supporting multilingual customer interactions.

Architecture

Below is high level architecture that we will follow-

  • BTP app (Node.js or CAP) exposes /api/translate → calls SAP Translation Hub REST API using an OAuth token (Destination service + XSUAA).
  • Simple web UI (UI5/HTML) lets the user choose source text/language and target language; it calls /api/translate, shows the result.
  • C4C  URL Mashup embeds the web UI.

         

 

 

 

Step 1 — Provision SAP Translation Hub on BTP

a) In BTP Cockpit, entitle and create a service instance of SAP Translation Hub, Free‑tier quotas are available for trying this out.

 

 

You are now able to create service bindings for your new service instance. Service bindings are used to generate credentials to enable apps to access and communicate with the service instance.

 

In the dialog, enter a binding name, such as binding01 the name of your service binding. Click Create button to create the service binding.

 

Download this file and copy values as highlighted above. They will be used in next step while creating destination.

clientid, clientsecret, url (API endpoint), and uaa.url (token endpoint).

 

b) Review the API access model (OAuth 2.0 client credentials) and the Translate method in the API reference.

 API Reference - Software Translation | SAP Help Portal

c) On api.sap.com, you can explore SAP Translation Hub and Document Translation endpoints and their request/response shapes.

Overview | SAP Translation Hub | SAP Business Accelerator Hub 

 

Step 2 — Create a BTP Destination for Translation Hub

Create a destination (e.g., STH_REST) so your backend can call Translation Hub without hard‑coding secrets:

Go to Connectivity → Destinations → New Destination.

 

Using destinations centralizes credentials and lets the Cloud SDK or approuter acquire tokens at runtime.

 

Note:

  • Use the uaa.url value from the service key plus /oauth/token appended — e.g. https://xxxxxxx.authentication.eu10.hana.ondemand.com/oauth/token.
  • Common mistake: pasting the bare uaa.url without /oauth/token, or using the url field (the API endpoint) instead of uaa.url (the auth endpoint) — these are two different keys in the service key JSON and easy to swap.
  • Set Token Service URL Type to Dedicated (not Common) unless you're deliberately sharing a token across multiple destinations.

 

Step 3 — Implement a tiny backend that calls Translation Hub

 

Below is a minimal Node.js (Express) route using the SAP Cloud SDK to call Translation Hub via the destination you created. (You can also do this in CAP; the principle is the same.)


Note: The exact payload of translate (fields like sourceLanguage, targetLanguages, textType, domain, units) is documented in the API ref; adapt the body to the Translate Method for API v2

 

// server.js
import express from 'express';
import cors from 'cors';
import { executeHttpRequest } from '@sap-cloud-sdk/http-client';

const app = express();
app.use(express.json());
app.use(cors());	

app.post('/api/translate', async (req, res) => {
  try {
    const { sourceLanguage, targetLanguage, text, domain, textType } = req.body;

    // Build API payload per SAP Translation Hub "translate" method (API v2)
    const payload = {
      sourceLanguage,
      targetLanguages: [targetLanguage],
      // optional depending on your use case:
      domain,        // e.g., "FIN", "CRM" – see “domains” method
      textType,      // e.g., "ui", "longtext" – see “texttypes” method
      units: [{ value: text }]
    };

    const response = await executeHttpRequest(
      { destinationName: 'STH_REST' }, // BTP Destination
      {
        method: 'POST',
        url: '/v2/translate',          // adjust if your base URL already includes /v2
        data: payload,
        headers: { 'Content-Type': 'application/json' }
      }
    );

    // Typical response has translations aligned to the requested targetLanguages
    res.json(response.data);
  } catch (err) {
    console.error(err);
    res.status(500).json({ error: 'Translation failed', details: `${err}` });
  }
});

const port = process.env.PORT || 4000;
app.listen(port, () => console.log(`Translation backend running on ${port}`));

 

Step 4 — Build a simple web UI (UI5 or plain HTML)

A small form that posts to /api/translate is enough. You can deploy the static UI and backend together to Cloud Foundry behind an approuter (HTML5 repo) and expose a single HTTPS URL.

 

<!-- public/index.html -->
<!doctype html>
<html lang="en">
<head>
  <meta charset="utf-8"/>
  <title>Translate (SAP Translation Hub)</title>
  <meta name="viewport" content="width=device-width, initial-scale=1"/>
  <style>
    body {
      font-family: "Arial", sans-serif;
      font-size: 14px;
      line-height: 1.4;
      padding: 20px;
      max-width: 800px;
      margin: 0 auto;
      color: #333;
    }
    h2 { margin-top: 0; }
    .form-group { margin-bottom: 15px; }
    label { display: inline-block; margin-right: 15px; font-weight: bold; }
    
    /* Updated styling for select and inputs */
    input, select { 
      padding: 6px; 
      border: 1px solid #ccc; 
      border-radius: 4px; 
      background-color: white;
    }
    
    textarea {
      width: 100%;
      padding: 8px;
      border: 1px solid #ccc;
      border-radius: 4px;
      box-sizing: border-box;
      resize: vertical;
    }
    button {
      background-color: #0070f2;
      color: white;
      border: none;
      padding: 10px 20px;
      border-radius: 4px;
      cursor: pointer;
      font-size: 14px;
    }
    button:hover { background-color: #005bb5; }
    button:disabled { background-color: #ccc; cursor: not-allowed; }
    #out {
      background: #f4f4f4;
      padding: 15px;
      border-radius: 4px;
      border: 1px solid #ddd;
      white-space: pre-wrap;
      margin-top: 20px;
      min-height: 50px;
    }
    .error { color: #d93025; }
  </style>
</head>
<body>
  <h2>Translate</h2>

  <div class="form-group">
    <label>Source language:
      <select id="srcLang">
        <option value="en" selected>English</option>
        <option value="zh">Chinese (Simplified)</option>
        <option value="es">Spanish</option>
        <option value="ar">Arabic</option>
        <option value="pt">Portuguese</option>
        <option value="fr">French</option>
        <option value="ja">Japanese</option>
        <option value="ru">Russian</option>
        <option value="de">German</option>
        <option value="hi">Hindi</option>
      </select>
    </label>
    
    <label>Target language:
      <select id="tgtLang">
        <option value="en">English</option>
        <option value="zh">Chinese (Simplified)</option>
        <option value="es">Spanish</option>
        <option value="ar">Arabic</option>
        <option value="pt">Portuguese</option>
        <option value="fr">French</option>
        <option value="ja">Japanese</option>
        <option value="ru">Russian</option>
        <option value="de" selected>German</option>
        <option value="hi">Hindi</option>
      </select>
    </label>
  </div>

  <div class="form-group">
    <textarea id="srcText" rows="5" placeholder="Enter text to translate..."></textarea>
  </div>

  <button id="go" type="button">Translate</button>

  <pre id="out"></pre>

  <script>
    // Read initial values from URL params
    const qp = new URLSearchParams(location.search);
    const srcTextInput = document.getElementById('srcText');
    const tgtLangInput = document.getElementById('tgtLang');
    const srcLangInput = document.getElementById('srcLang');
    const goBtn = document.getElementById('go');
    const outPre = document.getElementById('out');

    // Initialize fields from URL if present
    if (qp.get('text')) srcTextInput.value = qp.get('text');
    
    // If URL params provide a language code that exists in our list, select it
    if (qp.get('targetLang')) tgtLangInput.value = qp.get('targetLang');
    if (qp.get('sourceLang')) srcLangInput.value = qp.get('sourceLang');

    goBtn.addEventListener('click', async () => {
      // Reset output and set loading state
      outPre.textContent = '';
      outPre.classList.remove('error');
      goBtn.disabled = true;
      const originalBtnText = goBtn.textContent;
      goBtn.textContent = 'Translating...';

      try {
        const body = {
          sourceLanguage: srcLangInput.value,
          targetLanguage: tgtLangInput.value,
          text: srcTextInput.value,
          domain: qp.get('domain') || undefined,
          textType: qp.get('textType') || 'ui'
        };

        const response = await fetch('/api/translate', {
          method: 'POST',
          headers: { 'Content-Type': 'application/json' },
          body: JSON.stringify(body)
        });

        if (!response.ok) {
          throw new Error(`Server error: ${response.status} ${response.statusText}`);
        }

        const data = await response.json();
        outPre.textContent = JSON.stringify(data, null, 2);

      } catch (error) {
        console.error('Translation failed:', error);
        outPre.textContent = `Error: ${error.message}`;
        outPre.classList.add('error');
      } finally {
        // Restore button state
        goBtn.disabled = false;
        goBtn.textContent = originalBtnText;
      }
    });
  </script>
</body>
</html>

 

Step 5 — Deploy the app to BTP (Cloud Foundry)

 

  • Push the Node.js app (and static UI) to your CF space.
  • Ensure the Destination is bound/available.
  • Note the public URL (e.g., https://your-app.cfapps.eu10.hana.ondemand.com/).
  • The workshop’s sample shows the same flow (basic website + microservice) jens-limbach/SSv2-extensibility-workshop: SSv2-extensibility-workshop

 

Step 6 — Create the URL Mashup in C4C  and place it on a page

 

In C4C, go to Mashup Authoring → Create → URL Mashup.

          URL: paste your BTP app URL

Place the mashup: open a record (e.g., Ticket), Start Adaptation, add a new tab/section, drop the mashup, save. Here is the preview of the new mash up. I have used very basic HTML logic to create view; further modification can be done in HTML logic to blend in the view with C4C theme.

Note: SAP C4C V2 provides native, AI‑powered language translation. If needed this can be used in C4C V2 as well.

 

Conclusion

We have explored how SAP Cloud for Customer (C4C) can be enhanced with built-in translation capabilities using SAP Translation Hub on SAP Business Technology Platform (BTP). By implementing this solution, organizations can provide a seamless translation experience directly within C4C, enabling service agents to efficiently manage multilingual customer interactions, improve productivity, and deliver faster, more effective support.

I hope this guide helps you understand the end-to-end implementation approach and inspires further innovation in multilingual customer service scenarios. Should you have any questions, require clarification on any of the implementation steps, or wish to discuss alternative design approaches, please feel free to reach out. Your feedback and suggestions are highly valued and will help refine and strengthen this solution for broader adoption and future enhancements.

entry.title: How to Follow an HTTP Redirect in SAP CPI
entry.content:
The HTTP adapter does not follow redirects. Here is how to handle a 302 — and a trap that gives you a 403 on pre-signed URLs.

Many APIs answer a request with 302 Moved Temporarily and a Location header, instead of returning the data directly. The SAP CPI HTTP adapter does not follow that redirect automatically. This post shows how to handle it, and one trap that costs a lot of time when the redirect points to an AWS pre-signed URL.

TL;DR — CPI does not follow redirects. Read the Location header and call it again. But if that URL is a pre-signed URL (AWS S3/CloudFront, Azure SAS, ...), the HTTP adapter re-encodes it and you get a 403. The safe fix is to make that second call from a Groovy script with java.net.URL, which sends the URL exactly as it is.

Step 1: handle the redirect

The CPI HTTP adapter has no “follow redirects” option. By default it even throws an exception on a 3xx response. The standard way to handle it:

  1. On the first HTTP call, set Throw Exception on Failure = OFF.
  2. Copy the Location response header into a property (for example TargetLocation).
  3. Make a second HTTP call to that URL.

For most APIs this is enough. The problems start when the Location is a pre-signed URL.

The trap: a 403 on the second call

Here is a real example. The Basware API (public API behaviour, nothing customer-specific) returns the invoice attachment as a redirect:

GET /v1/accountingDocuments/{id}/attachments/{attachmentId}
-> 302, Location: https://api.us.basware.com/cfsecure/.../INVOICE%20%28copy%202%29%202026.pdf
   ?Expires=...&Signature=W-4m2LqUesX~Bxpzq6CfZ~Upf87...&Key-Pair-Id=...

The second call to that pre-signed URL fails with 403 Forbidden. But the same URL works in Postman or Bruno with “follow redirects” on. Why?

Turn on the trace and compare the Location you received with the URL CPI really sent (RealDestinationUrl). They are different:

Path:    received  ...INVOICE%20%28copy%202%29%202026.pdf
         CPI sent   ...INVOICE%20(copy%202)%202026.pdf      (%28/%29 decoded to "(" ")")
Query:   received  ...W-4m2LqUesX~Bxpzq6CfZ~Upf87...
         CPI sent   ...W-4m2LqUesX%7EBxpzq6CfZ%7EUpf87...   (every "~" encoded to %7E)

A pre-signed URL is signed over the exact bytes of the URL. If one character changes its encoding, the signature no longer matches, and you get a 403. Postman sends the URL exactly as it is. CPI changes it.

Note: this is not an AWS problem. It happens with any URL where the exact encoding must stay the same — AWS S3/CloudFront, Azure SAS tokens, Google Cloud signed URLs, and others.

Why CPI changes the URL

The answer is not in the SAP Help Portal — it is in the Apache Camel source code, which the HTTP adapter is built on. Two layers change the URL:

  • Camel UnsafeUriCharactersEncoder. Camel keeps a list ("BitSet") of characters it treats as unsafe and encodes them. The tilde is on that list (unsafeCharactersHttp.set('~') in the source), so every ~ becomes %7E.
  • Apache HttpClient. It normalizes the URI following RFC 3986. Parentheses are allowed in a path, so HttpClient decodes %28/%29 back to ( ).

Neither layer can be turned off in the adapter. If you want to check it yourself:

  • UnsafeUriCharactersEncoder: https://github.com/apache/camel/blob/main/core/camel-util/src/main/java/org/apache/camel/util/UnsafeUriCharactersEncoder.java
  • HttpProducer: https://github.com/apache/camel/blob/main/components/camel-http/src/main/java/org/apache/camel/component/http/HttpProducer.java

Reading the Camel source is a good habit — it answers questions the docs leave open.

What does not fix it

  • CamelHttpUri / CamelHttpQuery headers. They only change which URL goes into the adapter. The adapter still runs the same encoder. (I confirmed this with a trace: still 403.)
  • Adapter settings. There is no “raw URL” or “disable encoding” option.
  • The %25 escaping trick (SAP KBA 3131448, which I also described in an earlier post, "SAP Cloud Integration — URL escaping hack"). It helps with characters the adapter decodes (the parentheses): send %2528, and it stays %28. But it cannot help with the tilde, because the adapter always encodes ~ to %7E and there is no input that keeps a plain ~. So a configuration-only fix is not possible here.

The fix: call the URL from Groovy

Since no setting stops the encoding, make the second call outside the adapter — from a Groovy script with java.net.URL. new URL(str) sends the URL exactly as it is, like a browser or Postman.

Replace the second HTTP call (the Request-Reply step and its receiver channel) with one Groovy script step that reads the pre-signed URL from the property and downloads the file:

import com.sap.gateway.ip.core.customdev.util.Message
import java.net.HttpURLConnection
import java.net.URL

/**
 * Downloads a file from a pre-signed URL captured from the 302 "Location" header
 * (stored in property TargetLocation).
 *
 * Why Groovy and not the HTTP adapter:
 * The CPI HTTP adapter (Apache Camel + HttpClient) always re-encodes the URL - it
 * turns '~' into '%7E' and decodes '%28'/'%29' to '(' ')'. A pre-signed URL is
 * signed over the exact bytes, so any change -> 403. java.net.URL sends the URL
 * exactly as it is.
 */
def Message processData(Message message) {

    def messageLog = messageLogFactory.getMessageLog(message)
    def rawUrl = message.getProperty("TargetLocation")?.toString()

    // Guard: nothing to download
    if (rawUrl == null || rawUrl.trim().isEmpty()) {
        if (messageLog != null) {
            messageLog.addCustomHeaderProperty("Termination_Reason", "Missing_TargetLocation")
        }
        throw new IllegalStateException("TargetLocation is empty - no URL to download")
    }

    HttpURLConnection conn = null
    try {
        URL url = new URL(rawUrl)                    // keeps the URL byte-for-byte
        conn = (HttpURLConnection) url.openConnection()
        conn.setRequestMethod("GET")
        conn.setInstanceFollowRedirects(true)
        conn.setConnectTimeout(60000)
        conn.setReadTimeout(60000)
        // Pre-signed URL is self-authenticating -> no Authorization header.

        int rc = conn.getResponseCode()
        if (messageLog != null) {
            messageLog.addCustomHeaderProperty("Attachment_HTTP_Status", String.valueOf(rc))
        }

        if (rc >= 200 && rc < 300) {
            // Read the body in 8 KB chunks. Fine for normal documents;
            // for very large files, use a streaming pattern to avoid OOM.
            def buffer = new ByteArrayOutputStream()
            conn.getInputStream().withStream { input ->
                byte[] chunk = new byte[8192]
                int n
                while ((n = input.read(chunk)) != -1) {
                    buffer.write(chunk, 0, n)
                }
            }
            byte[] data = buffer.toByteArray()

            message.setBody(data)
            def ct = conn.getContentType()
            if (ct != null) { message.setHeader("Content-Type", ct) }
            message.setHeader("CamelHttpResponseCode", rc)

            if (messageLog != null) {
                messageLog.addCustomHeaderProperty("Attachment_Size_Bytes", String.valueOf(data.length))
            }
        } else {
            def err = ""
            try {
                def es = conn.getErrorStream()
                if (es != null) { es.withStream { err = it.getText("UTF-8") } }
            } catch (ignored) { }
            if (messageLog != null) {
                messageLog.addCustomHeaderProperty("Termination_Reason", "Download_Failed")
                messageLog.addCustomHeaderProperty("Error_Detail", "HTTP " + rc + ": " + err)
            }
            throw new RuntimeException("Download failed, HTTP " + rc + ": " + err)
        }
    } finally {
        if (conn != null) { conn.disconnect() }
    }

    return message
}

A note on using Groovy for HTTP

SAP recommends the HTTP adapter by default, and that is a good rule. But for a public pre-signed URL, the usual reasons against Groovy do not apply: no Cloud Connector, no client certificate, no proxy needed on Cloud Foundry. Two things still matter, and the script handles both: add MPL log entries so the call is visible in monitoring, and be careful with memory for very large files (stream instead of loading the full body).

Summary

  • The CPI HTTP adapter does not follow redirects — read the Location header and call again.
  • A 403 on a pre-signed URL is usually URL re-encoding, not authentication, and it is not AWS-specific.
  • The adapter re-encodes the URL in two layers (~ → %7E and %28 → (), and this cannot be turned off. When unsure, read the Camel source.
  • The %25 trick (KBA 3131448) fixes the parentheses but not the tilde.
  • To send the URL exactly as it is, use a Groovy script with java.net, and add MPL logs.

References

  • SAP KBA 3131448 — HTTP Receiver adapter discards encoding of special characters in address
  • SAP Cloud Integration — URL escaping hack (my earlier post): https://medium.com/@vbalko/sap-cloud-integration-url-escaping-hack-34a45f5a1f37
  • Apache Camel UnsafeUriCharactersEncoder: https://github.com/apache/camel/blob/main/core/camel-util/src/main/java/org/apache/camel/util/UnsafeUriCharactersEncoder.java
  • Apache Camel HttpProducer: https://github.com/apache/camel/blob/main/components/camel-http/src/main/java/org/apache/camel/component/http/HttpProducer.java

entry.title: AI Skills Library, CodeConnect, MCP Gateway, CDS-Caching 2.0 | SAP Developer News
entry.content:
Description

SAP AI Skills Library

  • https://skills.cloud.sap
  • Blog post: https://community.sap.com/t5/artificial-intelligence-blogs-posts/the-ai-skills-library-is-live-discover-share-and-build-ai-skills-together/ba-p/14442276
  • Tutorial: https://developers.sap.com/tutorials/ai-skills-library.html

Live from CodeConnect

  • Website: https://code-connect.dev/
  • UI5Con: https://openui5.org/ui5con/
  • reCAP: https://recap-conf.dev/
  • HANA Tech Con: https://hanatech.community/

API-centric Integration and MCP Gateway

  • API-Centric Integration in SAP Integration Suite: A new paradigm for API & Integration practitioner: https://community.sap.com/t5/technology-blog-posts-by-sap/api-centric-integration-in-sap-integration-suite-a-new-paradigm-for-api-amp/ba-p/14438245
  • MCP Gateway in SAP Integration Suite: Your APIs, Ready for the Age of Agents: https://community.sap.com/t5/technology-blog-posts-by-sap/mcp-gateway-in-sap-integration-suite-your-apis-ready-for-the-age-of-agents/ba-p/14438250
  • API-Centric Integration on SAP Integration Suite – Part 1: Build and Deploy Your API: https://community.sap.com/t5/technology-blog-posts-by-sap/api-centric-integration-on-sap-integration-suite-part-1-build-and-deploy/ba-p/14438357
  • API-Centric Integration on SAP Integration Suite – Part 2: API Governance with Developer Hub: https://community.sap.com/t5/technology-blog-posts-by-sap/api-centric-integration-on-sap-integration-suite-part-2-api-governance-with/ba-p/14438473

CDS-Caching 2.0

  • Announcement from Mike Zaschka https://www.linkedin.com/posts/mike-zaschka-7395949_sap-cap-cloudapplicationprogrammingmodel-share-7479651652631961600-kfa0/
  • GitHub: mikezaschka/cds-caching: Plugin for SAP CAP providing a powerful and flexible caching service

CHAPTER TITLES

00:00 Intro

00:10 SAP AI Skills Library

01:58 Live from CodeConnect

03:47 API-centric Integration and MCP Gateway

04:50 CDS-Caching 2.0

Transcription

00;00;01;00 - 00;00;06;09
Announcer
This is to develop a news for July 16th, 2026.

00;00;06;12 - 00;00;09;20
Nora von Thenen
Okay. I want to share something with you today that I think is really cool.

00;00;09;26 - 00;00;27;00
Nora von Thenen
It is called the AI Skills Library and you can find it under Skills<dot>Cloud<dot>SAP. And the reason why I think it's cool is because that is a collection of a lot of skills for an AI agent, right, that you can reuse from other people, but that you can also, for example, check out and learn from other people.

00;00;27;00 - 00;00;47;20
Nora von Thenen
But you don't only find skills, you can also, for example, find MCP servers and they're compatible with Joule Work and with Claude. So you can just import them into your system. And let's just have a look at how that works quickly. So yeah here for example you can find a skill for the CF CLI. So the Cloud Foundry CLI and there's the description for the skill.

00;00;47;20 - 00;01;08;17
Nora von Thenen
This is also how the agent that knows which skill to use when. And then here we can just import it to our Joule Work Desktop. And now it's actually importing the skill to my Joule Work okay. So we will update that. Great. And now you can see I have the CLI skill I had before the Joule A to agent skill and Joule CLI skill.

00;01;08;17 - 00;01;33;14
Nora von Thenen
I had those three imported before already and now I also have the CF CLI skill. Now you might wonder what is inside this skill. So you can check out the markdown file here. And it's really just a very very detailed description of how the CLI works, what the agent can use, which commands. For example, there's also a YAML file example somewhere here for the manifest YAML.

00;01;33;14 - 00;01;52;14
Nora von Thenen
And it's really just really detailed description of how to use the Cloud Foundry CLI, which is honestly helpful for humans and also very helpful for your agent. Whatever you're using to then just support you with using the Cloud Foundry CLI, for example. But there are so many different skills out there, and I really, really want to encourage you to create your own skills.

00;01;52;14 - 00;01;58;04
Nora von Thenen
Share them up here. And yeah, just let's contribute and reuse skills from one another.

00;01;58;06 - 00;02;17;20
Nicolai Schoenteich
Hi everyone. Welcome to the SAP Developer News. Were here at UI5Con in St. Leon/ROT and we had a fantastic day of sessions coding, connecting, talking to people. And we can't wait to come back next year. And we can't wait for the next events coming up as part of Code Connect tomorrow or the day after.

00;02;17;22 - 00;02;41;18
DJ Adams
So hello from reCAP 2026. It's Wednesday, the 15th of July. reCAP is well underway. It's been an awesome conference so far. Keynote from Daniel Hutzel, which really showed how central to AI for us and for you CAP really is. Anyway, we've had some great sessions. Looking forward to a nice evening event as well. See you soon.

00;02;41;21 - 00;03;00;07
Witalij Rudnicki
talking about AI, the concept of agents is not new. Right. Even though this book says artificial intelligence and modern approach, and this is the book where the agents were described for the very first time in the context of AI, how many of you know this book and know from which year it is.

00;03;00;09 - 00;03;02;15
Audience Member
74 ish?

00;03;02;17 - 00;03;11;14
Witalij Rudnicki
Now it's actually 1995 book. But yeah, from today's perspective, 74/95 sounds equally ancient.

00;03;11;17 - 00;03;16;21
Nora von Thenen
And our competitor SAP is also an alphabetical them as well.

00;03;16;22 - 00;03;46;26
Nora von Thenen
So that's pretty cool because then you can use all the interference to build your agents using the elements from their hand. And that's what I did today. And I was like a lightning. And then I obviously added a tool. I will show you the code for that in the second. And that tool is calling me. And then once you do this, agent two using the protocol, because then our information discovered by other agents and then we will call them agent.

00;03;46;29 - 00;03;50;06
Nora von Thenen
Cool.

00;03;50;09 - 00;04;20;22
Antonio Maradiaga
Holla, SAP developers. In case you missed it, the MCP gateway in SAP Integration Suite is now generally available. API centric integration is here. It is a new paradigm to how we create our API artifacts and MCP servers on the new Integration cell runtime. Think of it as designing the API and its mediation logic in one place. With the MCP gateway, we can turn our existing APIs into tools for AI agents with authentication, rate limiting, and traceability.

00;04;20;24 - 00;04;50;03
Antonio Maradiaga
Check out blog posts to learn more about API centric integration and the NCP gateway. Also, Ashutosh published a couple of blog posts in SAP community that works you end to end on how to build and deploy your first API on integration self, and then how to govern it through the Developer Hub. All of this available in the BTP trial, so you can go and check it out and get some hands on experience.

00;04;50;05 - 00;04;53;19
Antonio Maradiaga
Hasta luego.

00;04;53;22 - 00;05;19;10
Thomas Jung
And speaking of reCAP and all things CAP related. I wanted to point you towards the CDS-Caching project by Mike Zaschka. It's in its 2.0 version. He's just updated it for CAP 10. I can tell you I've already used it in a CAP 10 project. Found it very handy. I'm using it to catch some remote data that's frequently read.

00;05;19;12 - 00;05;44;28
Thomas Jung
Easy to set up, easy to use. Like so many of the CAP plugins. It's got settings for both memory based caching which would which is what I was using originally, but I had a multi server instance. So then all I had to do is flip a configuration and it used on a database based caching. So super easy to adjust with the configuration settings.

00;05;44;29 - 00;05;59;28
Thomas Jung
If you have a use case and many of the new features, as Mike points out, like the MCP server can really benefit for some caching. If you have such use cases, then definitely check out this great open source plug in to the CAP framework.

entry.title: Securing a CAP Node.js App with SAP AMS on BTP Cloud Foundry
entry.content:
Overview
 
This tutorial walks through building a CAP Node.js application, adding role-based access control using SAP Authorization Management Service (AMS) and SAP IAS, and deploying it to SAP BTP Cloud Foundry with MTA.
 
Prerequisites
  • SAP CAP SDK (@sap/cds-dk) installed globally
  • Cloud Foundry CLI (cf) installed
  • Access to an SAP BTP subaccount with:
    • SAP HANA Cloud instance
    • SAP IAS (Identity Authentication Service) tenant
    • SAP AMS (Authorization Management Service) entitlement
    • SAP Build Work Zone, standard edition (optional)

 

Procedure

 

Step 1 — Initialize the CAP Project

 

Create a new CAP Node.js project and add the built-in bookshop sample data model:
cds init capams --nodejs
cd capams
cds add sample

 
Step 2 — Add Authorization Annotations

 

Open srv/admin-service.cds and annotate the Books and Authors entities with @restrict to define role-based access:
annotate AdminService.Books with @(restrict: [
  {
    grant: ['READ'],
    to   : 'ManageAuthors'
  },
  {
    grant: ['READ', 'WRITE'],
    to   : 'ManageBooks'
  }
]);

annotate AdminService.Authors with @(restrict: [
  {
    grant: ['READ', 'WRITE'],
    to   : 'ManageAuthors'
  },
  {
    grant: ['READ'],
    to   : 'ManageBooks'
  }
]);

 

  • ManageAuthors can read/write Authors but only read Books
  • ManageBooks can read/write Books but only read Authors

 
Step 3 — Add IAS Authentication and AMS Authorization

 

Add SAP IAS as the authentication provider and SAP AMS for fine-grained authorization:
cds add ias
cds add ams

 

cds add ias adds "auth": "ias" under the [production] profile in package.json. cds add ams adds the @SAP/ams-dev dev dependency and wires up the AMS plugin.

 

Step 4 — Add HANA Cloud, MTA, and Work Zone Support
Configure SAP HANA Cloud as the production database, generate the MTA deployment descriptor, and add SAP Build Work Zone integration:
cds add hana
cds add mta
cds add workzone

 

cds add hana sets "model": "db/hana" for the production profile. cds add mta generates mta.yaml wiring all modules and service bindings. cds add workzone adds destination, html5-repo, workzone, and workzone-standard facets.

 

Step 5 — Deploy to Cloud Foundry

 

Log in to your BTP Cloud Foundry environment using SSO, then deploy the full MTA:
cf login -a <cloud-foundry-api-url> --sso
cds up

 

cds up compiles the AMS policies from the @restrict annotations, builds the MTA archive, and deploys all modules and services. Once complete, you will find the generated basePolicies.dcl under gen/policies/dcl/cap/:
POLICY "admin" {
    ASSIGN ROLE "admin";
}

POLICY "ManageAuthors" {
    ASSIGN ROLE "ManageAuthors";
}

POLICY "ManageBooks" {
    ASSIGN ROLE "ManageBooks";
}

 

Step 6 — Verify the Deployment

 

Service Bindings in BTP Cockpit

 

In the BTP Cockpit, navigate to your Cloud Foundry space, open the capams-srv application, and go to Service Bindings. Confirm that capams-ias (identity service, application plan) is bound.

 

 

Authorization Policies in SAP IAS

 

In the SAP IAS admin console, go to Applications & Resources → Applications, find the capams application, and open the Authorization Policies tab. The three policies generated from your annotations — admin, ManageAuthors, and ManageBooks — should appear under the cap package.
 

 

 

Click the admin policy to view its details and manage user assignments. You can assign users directly here.
 

 

 

 

 

 

Summary

 

Command Purpose

cds init capams --nodejsScaffold a new CAP Node.js project
cds add sampleAdd built-in bookshop sample data model
cds add iasConfigure IAS as the authentication provider
cds add amsAdd SAP AMS for authorization
cds add hanaSwitch production database to SAP HANA Cloud
cds add mtaGenerate the MTA deployment descriptor
cds add workzoneAdd SAP Build Work Zone integration
cds upBuild and deploy the full MTA to Cloud Foundry

 

The End! Thanks for your time! Jacky Liu

entry.title: 20 Years of Unbroken SAP Certification - How NTT DATA Turns Operations into a Strategic Edge
entry.content:
For more than 35 years, NTT DATA has been shaping how organizations run and transform their SAP landscapes – and their track record speaks for itself. The company has just received the Golden Certificate from SAP, marking their 10th consecutive certification and underscoring an unbroken commitment to quality that few partners can match. In an interview with Lars Janitz – Senior Executive and Head of Global Business & Managed Services at NTT DATA Business Solutions AG, and Head of SAP & ServiceNow for LE – he reveals how NTT DATA helps customers balance rock-solid operations with continuous innovation, without compromising business continuity. From SAP BTP and AI to global security operations, he explains where the greatest opportunities lie in the years ahead. Read on to find out why Lars Janitz believes that a strong operational foundation today is the key to unlocking the full potential of cloud and AI tomorrow.

Could you start by telling us about NTT DATA and the services you provide to customers worldwide?

NTT DATA is a global leader in SAP consulting, implementation, managed services, and business transformation. For more than 35 years, we have been supporting organizations in implementing and modernizing their SAP landscapes, increasing the value of the SAP landscape, moving to modern cloud platforms, and driving business innovation.

What sets us apart is our ability to combine global scale, deep SAP expertise, and local customer proximity. We support customers throughout the entire SAP lifecycle – from strategy and implementation to operations, optimization, and continuous innovation.

Handover of the golden certificate, July 2026

Looking back at your long partnership with SAP, what motivated NTT DATA to pursue its first SAP operations certification in Germany in 2001, and what were the key factors behind expanding that approach globally in 2006?

Our original motivation was simple: to provide customers with independent proof of the quality and reliability of our SAP operations services. As our customers expanded internationally, they expected the same level of service and governance everywhere they operated. Extending the certification approach globally allowed us to create a consistent delivery model, support multinational customers more effectively, and establish the foundation for the global managed services organization we have today.

Many customers are seeking both stability and innovation in their SAP landscapes. How does NTT DATA help customers balance reliable operations with ongoing transformation and modernization?

We don't see stability and innovation as conflicting objectives. Reliable operations and deep industry knowledge create the foundation for successful transformation. Our teams ensure that customers' SAP environments run securely and efficiently while continuously identifying opportunities to improve and automate processes, adopt cloud technologies, and leverage new SAP functionalities. This allows customers to modernize at their own pace without compromising business continuity.

 As SAP continues to accelerate cloud adoption, how is NTT DATA supporting customers on their transformation journey today, and where do you see technologies such as SAP BTP and AI creating the greatest opportunities over the next several years?

We help customers accelerate their cloud journey through a combination of advisory, implementation, managed services, and ongoing optimization. Our goal is to deliver business value, not just technical migration. Furthermore, we leverage our 360-degree partnership with SAP to enhance and innovate our services. 

Looking ahead, SAP BTP and AI will play a critical role in helping organizations integrate processes, extend applications, automate decisions, and unlock greater productivity. The biggest opportunity lies in turning trusted business data into actionable intelligence.

NTT DATA recently achieved global certification for SAP Business AI and SAP security operations. How do these capabilities complement your existing managed services portfolio, and what benefits are customers already seeing today?

These certifications are a natural next step in the evolution of our managed services portfolio. They strengthen our ability to operate SAP environments at scale, intelligently, and securely. For customers, this means faster adoption of AI technologies, stronger security governance, and greater confidence that innovation can be introduced without increasing operational risk.

How do you experience the audits in practice, and what value do they bring to your teams and customers?

We view audits as an opportunity to improve rather than simply a compliance requirement. They provide valuable external validation and help us continuously strengthen our processes, capabilities, and delivery standards. For customers, they provide assurance that our services are regularly assessed against rigorous SAP requirements and industry best practices.

Can you share an example of an insight or recommendation from a past audit that led to a meaningful improvement in the way you deliver services or engage with customers?

One recurring theme has been the importance of globally aligned service management combined with strong local execution. Those insights encouraged us to further standardize incident management, reporting, and governance across our delivery organization. As a result, customers benefit from greater transparency, more consistent service experiences, and improved responsiveness across regions.

With a broad portfolio of SAP operations certifications across multiple solutions and regions, what does this global consistency mean for customers in their day-to-day operations and transformation projects?

For customers, global consistency means trust and predictability. They know what level of service, governance, and expertise they can expect regardless of location. Whether they are running business-critical operations or executing a major transformation program, our standardized delivery approach helps reduce complexity, mitigate risk, and accelerate outcomes.

Looking ahead, how do you expect demand for SAP Business AI operations to evolve, and how is NTT DATA preparing its managed services organization to support that evolution globally?

We expect demand to increase rapidly as AI moves from experimentation to enterprise-wide adoption. Customers will increasingly need partners that can not only deploy AI but also operate and govern it effectively. We are investing in AI operations capabilities, delivery models, security, and talent development to help customers confidently and responsibly scale SAP Business AI across their organizations.

What advice would you give to organizations that are currently defining their SAP operations strategy for the years ahead?

Our advice is to view operations as a strategic enabler of innovation. A modern operations strategy should balance resilience, security, and efficiency with the ability to rapidly adopt new technologies and provide valuable outcomes. Organizations that build a strong operational foundation today will be much better positioned to take advantage of cloud, AI, and future SAP innovations tomorrow.

 

Information on the SAP certification program for operations partners, as well as an overview of all certified partners, can be found in the SAP Operations Partner Guide.

Visit OPC at SAP.com or on the SAP Partner Portal.

entry.title: The refreshed SAP Integration Certification Framework is now available
entry.content:
After months of close collaboration with our partner ecosystem, the SAP Integration and Certification Center (ICC) is proud to announce that the refreshed SAP Integration Certification Framework is officially live now.

This is a significant milestone — not just for SAP ICC, but for every partner building solutions on the SAP platform. The refreshed framework is the result of real conversations, real feedback, and a shared commitment to making integration certification more accessible, more relevant, and more aligned with where SAP and its partners are heading.

Why We Refreshed the Framework

The integration landscape has changed fundamentally. The rise of Business AI, the shift to Clean Core, the evolution of SAP Business AI Platform, and the introduction of new API policy all demanded a certification framework that could keep pace.

The previous framework served us well — but it was built for a different era. We set out to build something that reflects today's partner reality: diverse solution types, varied maturity levels, diverse system landscapes and an ecosystem that spans from established global partners to innovative niche specialists entering the market.

The result is a framework that is structured yet flexible, rigorous yet accessible.

Two Paths. One Goal.

At the heart of the refreshed framework is a dual-path model — two transparent paths designed to meet partners where they are.

SAP Partner Solution Certification Framework 2026 — Two transparent paths

Of the two pathways, Integration Certification is more closely aligned with SAP product architecture strategy. It is designed for applications built on SAP Business AI Platform that meet a defined set of technical standards: use of published APIs, adherence to Clean Core principles, connectivity to SAP Business Data Cloud, and — where applicable — built with SAP Business AI.

Integration Certification is based on endorsed use-cases and technical criteria by SAP. SAP ICC certifies against a defined portfolio of scenarios available at SAP Partner Portal, meaning the partner application shall map to one of those scenarios to be eligible.

The updated framework is aligned with SAP API policy. A certified solution is not just technically validated — it is a direct confirmation that the solution adheres to the design time guidelines of the new SAP API policy, giving customers and partners a clear and trusted signal of compliance.

Successfully certified solutions receive the SAP Certified logo and a certificate, are listed in the SAP Certified Solutions Directory, and gain access to go-to-market support through SAP Store — making certification not just a quality mark, but a tangible business advantage. Integration Certification continues to be a mandatory requirement for higher solution progression levels in SAP PartnerEdge (Spotlight+, Endorsed App).

Interoperability Review is a new, purposeful entry point into the integration certification framework — and one of the most requested suggestion from our partner and the broader community. Certain partner solutions are not able to qualify for Integration Certification and fit a predefined certification scenario. That's not a dead end – it's what the Interoperability Review is built for.

Interoperability Review is SAP ICC's open and inclusive validation path, available to partners and non-partners (ISVs) alike, regardless of where you sit in the SAP ecosystem. It is designed for applications that use published APIs correctly but integrate via methods or platforms that fall outside the SAP Integration Certification Framework – and it's open to standard, market-ready products.

SAP ICC assesses your application against interoperability standards: correct use of published APIs as documented, successful authentication and connectivity, correct data exchange, and appropriate error handling. The review confirms technical compatibility – without requiring deep platform alignment.

If the partner solution passes the Interoperability Review, the solution is listed as interoperable on SAP Notes, and receive a Review Summary documenting the outcome of the technical assessment. With these outcomes, partners can demonstrate to joint customers that the solution is compatible with SAP suite of solutions.

These two paths are not competing options. They are complementary instruments that together ensure every partner has a supported route to market for their solution through a technical validation by SAP.

What Happens Next

The framework is now live. Here is how to get started:

  1. Explore the two paths — review the full documentation ICC pages at SAP.com and SAP Partner Portal
  2. Understand the Certification Framework Reference Guide and FAQ
  3. Talk to us — contact the ICC team (icc@sap.com) to discuss which path is the right fit for your solution or apply for our service
  4. Start your journey — the process begins with a short discovery conversation with the ICC team.

A Thank You to Our Partner Community

This framework did not emerge from a room at SAP. It emerged from the conversations we have had with many partners — at partner events, in certification reviews, in feedback sessions, and from the direct input you have shared with us over the years.

To everyone who participated in shaping this: thank you. You will see your contribution reflected in what we have built with the refreshed framework.

We are proud of this framework. We are more proud of what it makes possible for you and your customers. We at SAP ICC are eager to see it go live and really making a change for our partner community.

The SAP ICC Team
SAP Integration and Certification Center

Have questions? Reach out to the ICC team at icc@sap.com

entry.title: The Board Decides, the CIO Delivers: A Digital Health Platform Powered by SAP
entry.content:
In my previous posts I described SAP’s global healthcare industry strategy, a modular, data-driven, open, and interoperable platform, and I argued that a digital health platform (DHP) is ultimately a business decision that belongs on the CEO’s desk. Once the board has made that call, however, the mandate lies with the CIO, who turns a strategic ambition into a governed and sequenced delivery program that runs alongside daily operations without disrupting them. This post is written for that very role, and it tries to focus on two questions: what does a digital health platform powered by SAP actually consist of, and why does it make the CIO’s task easier instead of adding yet another program to an already full portfolio?

A landscape grown by change, not by design

In my experience, most healthcare providers definitely do not suffer from a lack of applications; they suffer from a system landscape that has grown over many years and rarely by design, and the figures show this: a symplr survey of health system clinicians and CIOs found that a majority of organizations run somewhere between 50 and 500, sometimes even more, different software solutions to operate their healthcare business, and in the United States alone the sector saw more than 900 hospital merger and acquisition transactions between 2014 and 2024, each of which brought their own systems, teams, and technical debt into somebody else’s landscape. So, there is an electronic medical record (EMR) system, or several of them after mergers, one or more revenue cycle management (RCM) systems for billing and patient accounting, a sometimes aging enterprise resource planning (ERP) system, a number of departmental specialty systems, research databases, and a long tail of point solutions, each of which brings its own data model, its own integration approach, and its own vendor release schedule. In such a landscape, every new capability becomes its own project, and every project has to solve integration, data quality, and compliance all over again, and the pace of change of the whole institution is effectively set by whichever vendor moves slowest.

The timing pressure is real, too. Many hospitals still run SAP Business Suite 7 (ECC), whose mainstream maintenance ends in 2027, with extended maintenance through 2030 and a Transition Option in SAP Private Cloud through 2033, and in Europe the secondary-use provisions of the European Health Data Space (EHDS) Regulation begin to apply in March 2029, which means the CIO’s delivery window for both obligations is essentially the same three to four years. And the platform market itself is being shaped in exactly that window: Gartner estimated in its April 2024 research "Innovation Insight: Digital Health Platforms Accelerate Transformation" that the digital health platform market was only around five percent penetrated, with adoption expected to play out over five to ten years, so the institutions making their architectural choices now are the ones whose requirements the market will form around.

The EHR-first instinct, and where it ends

Before laying out our answer, let me name the objection I hear most often, because it deserves a serious analysis. Many health systems reasonably prefer to look at their EMR vendor first for any new capability; successive Bain and KLAS surveys have found that roughly two out of three look first to their existing vendors, and especially their EMR vendor, before considering anyone else. I understand the instinct, since the EMR carries the clinical workflows and much of the clinical data, and nobody wants another vendor relationship without good reason. Yet the same market research paints a sobering picture of how that instinct works out in practice, with only about a quarter of executives in a Sage Growth survey saying their EMR vendor fully lives up to its promises. The deeper issue, though, is not vendor performance but scope: the decisions a CIO is now asked to support, from cost transparency per procedure to workforce planning to supply chain resilience to research data provisioning, depend on finance, procurement, workforce, and logistics data that does not live in the EMR and never will, and a system built to document and bill for care was simply never designed to be the enterprise data and AI foundation of a health system. This is, incidentally, also where the analyst community has landed: Gartner has addressed its digital health platform research explicitly to healthcare provider CIOs since its initial Market Guide in 2021, describing a shift away from monolithic, EMR-centric application suites towards a platform architecture, and in its most recent framing "Digital Health Platforms (Transitioning to Healthcare Provider Industry Cloud Platforms) Reviews and Ratings" positions the category as evolving into Healthcare Provider Industry Cloud Platforms whose purpose is to let a CIO adjust the application portfolio at the pace of business change. That last phrase describes the job better than I ever could.

Connect, govern, innovate, transform

Our strategy for the digital health platform powered by SAP addresses exactly this in four dimensions: connect, govern, innovate, and transform. Let me take them in the order an actual delivery program would.

To connect means to bring disparate systems into a single, coherent view across clinical and enterprise operations. The platform speaks the standards your landscape already uses, among them HL7 FHIR®, openEHR, IHE (Integrating the Healthcare Enterprise), OMOP (the common data model of the Observational Medical Outcomes Partnership), and, in Germany, ISiK as the national interoperability standard for hospital information systems. It connects EMR and revenue cycle management systems as well as EHDS-related sources, alongside SAP and non-SAP applications, whether they run as software as a service, cloud-native, hybrid, or on-premises. The AnyEMR/MultiEMR and AnyRCM/MultiRCM principles I described in the strategy post apply here, too: the platform integrates across whatever clinical and billing systems you have chosen rather than locking you into a single vendor’s stack, and since SAP does not build EMR or RCM systems itself, we unify across them together with and through our partner ecosystem.

To govern is, for the CIO, the most consequential of the four: in most institutions today, governance is a per-project exercise: every integration project defines its own access rules, every research request triggers its own anonymization effort, every audit finding produces its own remediation plan, and the sum of all this is a patchwork of controls that nobody can fully describe, let alone defend under time pressure. On the platform, governance becomes a managed capability instead. Master data governance, identity and access, consent and rights management, pseudonymization and anonymization, and data sovereignty compliance are built into the architecture and travel with every data product, so a rule defined once, for example who may see identifiable patient data and under which consent, is enforced wherever that data flows, whether into an operational dashboard, an AI model, or a research extract. That does not make audit readiness automatic, because audits will always also examine processes and people, but it makes audit readiness sustainable, since the technical controls no longer have to be reconstructed and re-documented for every new use of the data. And where residency requirements demand it, the same architecture can be deployed on sovereign-cloud options, so that the jurisdiction question is answered by design rather than negotiated per project. This is also where regulation stops being only a burden: an institution whose consent, provenance, and de-identification are managed capabilities enters the EHDS era prepared, while one that handles them project by project will spend those same years catching up.

To innovate then builds on that foundation rather than around it: a composition layer with analytics from descriptive to predictive, knowledge graphs for reasoning across clinical, operational, and financial domains, healthcare-specific semantics and ontologies, which are expanding to include terminologies such as SNOMED CT and the OMOP data models, and AI domain models including clinical natural language processing, all accessible through build tools from low-code to pro-code, so that extensions live safely outside a clean core and survive the next upgrade, a lesson many of us learned the hard way in the ERP world. And to transform is simply what follows when the first three are in place: decisions powered by governed data and embedded AI, at a pace the institution sets for itself.

The architecture, seen from the delivery side

At the top level, the DHP architecture consists of three layers, and increasingly a fourth. A data foundation provides clinical, enterprise, and ecosystem connectivity, including the healthcare interoperability standards mentioned above. A digital innovation layer carries the intelligence and the health-specific business context. A healthcare experiences layer offers applications such as patient portals, clinical workplaces, health apps and dashboards, and scenarios like clinical trial matching, patient identity resolution, and the EHDS health record. And on top of these sits the AI layer, in which embedded intelligence, assistants, and increasingly agentic capabilities operate on the governed layers beneath them rather than beside them. Readers who know Gartner’s published DHP reference architecture from their "Market Guide for Digital Health Platforms" will recognize the shape of the first three: an integrated data fabric with health data connectors, data curation and enrichment, metadata management, a healthcare-specific data model, and privacy and consent controls; a layer of packaged business capabilities; and a composition layer with low-code tooling for building tailored experiences. Our architecture is a concrete instantiation of exactly that pattern, and it deliberately extends it in the one dimension that has moved fastest since the pattern was first described: the AI layer on top. And for readers who want to explore how this platform category is developing across the wider market, Vince Kuraitis’s series “Platforming Healthcare – The Long View” offers a thorough long-term perspective.

What binds these layers together is the data product, and it comes in two kinds. Primary data products are sourced directly from a clinical or enterprise application and represent the original, unprocessed data: claims records from the revenue cycle management system, encounter summaries from the EMR, patient records from a clinical data repository (CDR), or financials from SAP Cloud ERP. Derived data products are curated and enriched on top of them, and here, health-specific content such as Patient 360, treatment cost, clinical workforce readiness, and patient risk scores, which deliver ready-to-use insight without additional integration effort, are being built out. Anyone who has run a hospital integration program knows why this matters: instead of maintaining n×m point-to-point integrations between sources and consumers, you build governed, reusable assets once and consume them everywhere, with lineage and policy enforced from the start.

This is what that AI layer changes in practice: because it is grounded in exactly this governed, contextualized data, and because it acts through the platform’s managed capabilities rather than around them, AI can be deployed where it demonstrably relieves pressure in daily operations. SAP Joule, our AI assistant, is being extended by SAP and partners with healthcare scenarios to address the everyday pain points of running a hospital, from clinical documentation, coding and DRG optimization, and denial management to medication replenishment, patient scheduling, regulatory reporting, and workforce planning, while differentiating industry scenarios around Patient 360, clinical workforce optimization, and denial prevention and revenue recovery build on the very same foundation. The CIO needs neither a separate AI stack nor a separate governance regime for it, and no separate integration program to feed it, because the platform itself is the AI foundation. It is also the path to what we at SAP call the Autonomous Enterprise: operations in which routine work increasingly runs itself, under the institution's own rules and controls. That kind of autonomy cannot be installed; it becomes possible only once connected data, business context, and governance are already in place, which is exactly what the layers beneath the AI provide.

That this approach works in practice is not a hypothesis: Hirslanden, Switzerland’s largest private hospital group, modernized its core processes and systems to support new clinical practices, regulatory standards, and cost reimbursement using SAP Signavio business transformation solutions, an example of how process intelligence grounded in a common platform accelerates transformation in healthcare rather than adding to the project load.

An ecosystem by design

No single vendor covers healthcare end to end, and we at SAP do not pretend to. Partners extend the platform with the clinical capabilities and industry solutions, built on SAP Business Technology Platform and SAP Business Data Cloud, delivered through SAP PartnerEdge, and available via SAP Store. For the CIO, that translates into freedom of choice at the edges and a stable, standards-based core in the middle.

A pragmatic sequence, not a big bang

Given the maintenance and regulatory horizons described above, a deliberate sequence is in fact the lower-risk path. It begins with mapping the landscape and agreeing on three to five cross-functional use cases that span clinical and operational domains. It continues with setting up the data foundation and publishing the first data products with governance in place, and with modernizing the ERP core at the institution’s own pace, be it with GROW with SAP for first-time adopters or with RISE with SAP for existing landscapes, where most providers favor separating the ERP and hospital information system transformations, with ERP first, to harmonize master data before clinical systems are subsequently integrated. It then connects the EMR, RCM, and priority partner solutions through standards-based integration including HL7 FHIR®, and it proves value early with time-boxed pilots before scaling responsibly.

The outcome is what the board asked for in the first place, and it is what running best means in healthcare: an institution that adopts innovation at its own speed, meets its regulatory obligations by design, and, where it chooses to, takes part in emerging health data collaborations on its own terms. I will not promise any CIO that a platform shrinks the portfolio overnight, because none does, and every practitioner knows that platforms are additive in the first place before they are consolidating. What it does provide is something more important: an operational model with a credible, governed path from a landscape that dictates its own terms to one that follows yours.

entry.title: Summer Learning Spotlight: Discovering Joule Studio
entry.content:
The summer months can be a great opportunity to step back from daily project work and invest some time in learning something new.

As SAP Business AI continues to evolve, I find it increasingly interesting to explore the technologies that help bring AI closer to business users. One topic that recently caught my attention is Joule Studio.

What I enjoy most about learning is that it doesn't always have to be tied to a certification or a specific project. Sometimes it's simply about discovering a new capability, understanding its potential, and gaining inspiration for future use cases.

That's why Joule Studio is currently on my learning list. If you're looking for a new topic to explore this summer, the following SAP Learning course might be a good starting point:

👉 https://learning.sap.com/courses/introducing-joule-studio

What is currently on your learning list? I'd love to hear which SAP technologies or AI topics you're exploring this summer.
